source.title: Pricing + usage review with Acme AI
topics: Invoice tokens vs Usage API export mismatch
Billed tokens vs raw tokens definition
Proposed per-request export format + timezone alignment
Dispute process and next reconciliation steps
source.summary: Acme AI flagged a discrepancy between Feb invoice preview and their internal counts/usage exports (invoice billed tokens higher). Redwood explained the current Hosted API metering semantics (billed_tokens derived from prompt+completion tokens, model tokenizer-specific; streaming bills for tokens actually generated; retries/fallback should not double-bill but there is a known edge case under certain server retry paths). Nadia confirmed they are rolling out improved dedupe/idempotency and cache_hit attribution fields, plus a "shadow billing compare" to validate changes before they affect invoices. The group agreed on a standard daily export schema (UTC) including request_id, idempotency_key, model, region, raw vs billed tokens, cache_hit fields, retry/fallback indicators, and status so Acme can reconcile per-request. Redwood to run a targeted reconciliation for 2026-02-01 through 2026-02-05 and follow up with findings and any credit recommendation if warranted.
next_steps: Redwood (Nadia) to send the internal meter definition for billed_tokens vs raw_tokens and streaming/retry rules to Acme (email).
Redwood to generate a per-request usage export for Acme for 2026-02-01 through 2026-02-05 in UTC with agreed fields.
Acme (Mark) to share two concrete examples (request_id + timestamp) where they believe retries were double-charged.
Schedule a 30-minute follow-up early next week to review the export + reconcile deltas and decide on remediation/credit if any.
action_items: Owner: Nadia Rahman — Provide Acme daily per-request export (UTC) with request_id, idempotency_key, model, region, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, raw_tokens, billed_tokens, cache_hit, cached_prompt_tokens, retry_count, fallback_model, http_status, created_at — Due: 2026-02-09
Owner: Jordan Blake — Email Acme the billing/metering FAQ + dispute workflow and confirm invoice preview timing — Due: 2026-02-07
Owner: Mark Liu — Send 2–3 sample request_ids suspected of double-billing + their internal token calc method — Due: 2026-02-07
Owner: Priya Desai — Confirm whether traffic is via OpenAI-compatible endpoint vs native Redwood endpoint for the affected workloads — Due: 2026-02-07
source.transcript:
Meeting: Pricing + usage review with Acme AI
Recorded: 2026-02-06 16:00 PM (PT)
Duration: 49 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake, Nadia Rahman, Priya Desai, Mark Liu, Sara Kim

[00:00] Jordan Blake: Cool, hey everybody. Thanks for making the time. I know this is a little bit of a fire drill because of the invoice preview. Goal today is to get super concrete on what you’re seeing, align on what “billed tokens” means on our side, and then agree on an export format so you can reconcile without guessing.

[00:18] Mark Liu: Yep, thanks Jordan. We basically have two numbers and they don’t match. Our internal meter from logs says ~1.12 billion tokens for the period, and your preview shows 1.28. And it’s not like rounding. It’s like… a lot.

[00:33] Priya Desai: And we pulled your usage API daily export, and that also doesn’t line up with the invoice preview, which is the weird part. Like, which one is source of truth.

[00:45] Jordan Blake: Got it. Nadia’s on, she owns a lot of the metering definitions. Nadia, do you want to set context on what the invoice preview is pulling from?

[00:54] Nadia Rahman: Yeah. So invoice preview and the final invoice are based on the billing ledger aggregation, which is computed from usage events. The customer usage API is also based on usage events, but it’s a different aggregation path and there are a couple fields we recently added that weren’t always present, like cache hit attribution. That can create “why is this number different” if you’re comparing two snapshots at different times or with different dimensions.

[01:19] Sara Kim: So the usage API can be wrong?

[01:22] Nadia Rahman: I wouldn’t say wrong, but it can lag, and there have been cases where some dimensions were missing and later backfilled. The ledger is our source for RevRec and invoicing.

[01:33] Mark Liu: That’s exactly the concern. We need a number we can audit. Not “maybe it lags.”

[01:40] Jordan Blake: Totally fair. And that’s on us to make it auditable. Let’s first isolate whether this is definition mismatch versus an actual bug like double counting retries.

[01:53] Priya Desai: I can say we suspect retries. We see timeouts, then we retry on our side with the same idempotency key. Also I think your system does fallback sometimes?

[02:05] Nadia Rahman: We do server-side fallback in a few scenarios, like capacity constraints or transient GPU errors. The intended behavior is: one logical request should bill once. That means dedupe should happen based on request_id and idempotency key.

[02:22] Mark Liu: Intended is doing a lot of work there.

[02:26] Nadia Rahman: Yeah, sorry. There is a known edge case specifically with streaming + server retry, where a partial stream gets emitted, then the server replays with a fallback model and we can end up with two usage events if the dedupe key isn’t stable across the retry path. That’s something we’ve been fixing.

[02:47] Jordan Blake: And to be clear, we’re not asking you to pay for a bug. We just need to validate if that’s what’s happening here.

[03:00] Sara Kim: Can we step back to the tokens definition? Because even our raw token numbers don’t match sometimes.

[03:07] Nadia Rahman: Sure. For Hosted API, tokens are counted with the model’s tokenizer. “prompt_tokens” are tokens for the input messages, “completion_tokens” are output tokens actually generated. “raw_tokens” is prompt + completion. “billed_tokens” may differ from raw in two cases: caching and policy adjustments.

[03:27] Priya Desai: What’s policy adjustments.

[03:29] Nadia Rahman: Things like minimum billing increments for certain endpoints, or when there’s a tool call serialization—like structured output—where the server adds wrapper tokens. To be honest, that’s usually small. The bigger delta tends to be caching or retries.

[03:48] Mark Liu: We are using your OpenAI compatible endpoint. Could that be part of it? We tokenize locally with tiktoken.

[03:56] Nadia Rahman: Yeah, that can be part of it. OpenAI-compatible endpoint is compatible at the API level, but token counting depends on the exact model. If you’re calling, say, “rw-llama-70b-instruct,” tokenization will not match OpenAI’s cl100k_base. If you’re using tiktoken from OpenAI, it will differ.

[04:16] Priya Desai: But your usage export claims “prompt_tokens” and “completion_tokens.” We’re not trying to recalc those, we’re trying to reconcile your export to your invoice.

[04:24] Nadia Rahman: Right. And that’s why the export needs to include both raw and billed, plus the dedupe identifiers.

[04:33] Jordan Blake: Mark, can you describe how you computed 1.12B? Is it from our response usage fields, or is it from your own tokenizer?

[04:41] Mark Liu: Mix. We sum your response usage fields per request as returned in the API response. For some older requests we didn’t store it, so we approximate from logs. But for the period in question, we stored the usage object.

[04:56] Nadia Rahman: OK, that helps. If you’re summing response usage fields, those should match usage events closely. Unless there’s duplication downstream or late arriving events.

[05:09] Priya Desai: Late arriving events meaning you bill later than the day?

[05:14] Nadia Rahman: Telemetry can arrive late if there are pipeline retries. We have SLAs for freshness, but there are cases where a day’s total changes slightly after backfill.

[05:26] Mark Liu: Slightly is fine. This is like 14 percent.

[05:30] Jordan Blake: Yeah. Let’s focus on the 14%.

[05:36] Nadia Rahman: The two suspects I’d start with:
1) duplicate usage events for the same logical request (idempotency/dedupe regression), and
2) cache_hit attribution missing. If cache hits are present but not attributed, it could look like billed tokens are higher than they should be.

[05:56] Sara Kim: Wait, caching reduces billed tokens?

[05:59] Nadia Rahman: In our system, yes, if prefix caching is enabled and we can attribute cached prompt tokens, then billed_tokens excludes the cached portion. If the cache_hit fields are missing, billed_tokens may default to raw.

[06:14] Priya Desai: We didn’t even know caching was on.

[06:16] Jordan Blake: It’s on by default in some tiers, and it’s workload-dependent. But we should have been clearer.

[06:23] Mark Liu: That’s kind of annoying because from our perspective it’s like, you changed the meter.

[06:29] Nadia Rahman: Totally. To be precise: caching doesn’t change the raw token count; it changes what you pay for if we can attribute it. The issue is when the attribution is inconsistent.

[06:44] Priya Desai: Okay. So how do we prove which one it is, retries or cache attribution.

[06:50] Nadia Rahman: We need per-request rows. Right now your daily export is aggregated. If we give you a per-request export with request_id and idempotency_key, you can dedupe and also see which requests had fallback_model populated, retry_count, and cache_hit.

[07:09] Mark Liu: Can you actually give us that? Like a CSV.

[07:12] Jordan Blake: Yes, with some constraints around PII. But we can provide request-level usage with metadata without content.

[07:20] Sara Kim: Also timezone. Your daily export is… I think it’s UTC but it doesn’t say.

[07:26] Nadia Rahman: It’s currently UTC, but I agree it’s not super explicit. In the export we send you, we’ll include created_at in ISO and the partition_date as UTC.

[07:38] Priya Desai: Great. I also want to understand streaming. We use stream=true and sometimes clients disconnect. Are we billed for tokens that were generated but not delivered?

[07:49] Nadia Rahman: You’re billed for tokens generated. If the server already generated tokens and sent chunks, that’s usage. If the client disconnects, generation may stop, but it can depend on timing. There’s not a concept of “delivered tokens only,” it’s “generated tokens.”

[08:07] Mark Liu: That could explain some, but not 14%. Disconnects aren’t that common.

[08:14] Jordan Blake: Right.

[08:16] Nadia Rahman: On retries: If you retry with the same idempotency key, you should not be billed twice. If you retry without it, you will be billed twice because it looks like a new request.

[08:28] Priya Desai: We do send an idempotency key. But I’m not 100% sure it’s consistent across all services.

[08:34] Sara Kim: We have two clients. One is Python, one is TS.

[08:38] Nadia Rahman: Got it. There was also a change in our SDK recently where idempotency header name got normalized. I don’t know if you’re on the latest.

[08:50] Priya Desai: We pinned version from like November.

[08:53] Jordan Blake: Okay, that’s likely part of it. Not the whole delta, but it’s a thing.

[09:00] Nadia Rahman: We can include in the export whether idempotency_key is present, and also a computed “dedupe_key” we use internally.

[09:10] Mark Liu: Please do.

[09:12] Jordan Blake: Let’s align on export fields. I’ll propose and you tell us if it works.

[09:18] Jordan Blake: Fields: created_at, request_id, idempotency_key, account_id, project_id (if you have it), endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings), model, region, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, raw_tokens, billed_tokens, cache_hit true/false, cached_prompt_tokens, retry_count, fallback_model (if any), http_status, and maybe “streaming” flag.

[09:44] Mark Liu: Add “request_cost_usd” if possible.

[09:47] Nadia Rahman: We can add unit price and extended cost, yes. It’s computed from billed_tokens * rate for that model/plan.

[09:55] Priya Desai: And can we get “error_type” or something. Because a lot of the weirdness is timeouts.

[10:01] Nadia Rahman: We can include an error_code dimension, but it’s not always present. Sometimes it’s just http_status and an internal reason.

[10:10] Sara Kim: That’s fine.

[10:12] Mark Liu: Also we need it daily, not just for this one period.

[10:16] Jordan Blake: Let’s do the one period first to reconcile, then we can talk about ongoing. The product is adding more robust usage exports in general.

[10:24] Nadia Rahman: Yeah, longer-term the usage API will surface billed_tokens explicitly, plus cache_hit fields, so you won’t need ad-hoc exports.

[10:35] Priya Desai: The invoice preview currently is just a number, right? We can’t see request breakdown.

[10:40] Jordan Blake: Correct, today. That’s why we’re doing this.

[10:46] Mark Liu: Okay. On the discrepancy specifically, can we talk about the dates? It’s Feb 1 to Feb 5. That’s what preview covers.

[10:55] Nadia Rahman: The preview is month-to-date. For Feb 1–5 we can run a ledger vs usage-events compare.

[11:03] Jordan Blake: Mark, do you have a couple request IDs where you saw a retry, and you think you got billed twice?

[11:09] Mark Liu: We have examples, but we don’t have request_id in your dashboard. We only have our own correlation id.

[11:16] Priya Desai: We can log your x-request-id header. I think it’s in response headers.

[11:20] Nadia Rahman: Yes, x-redwood-request-id. If you can send 2 or 3 of those, we can trace in the event stream.

[11:30] Mark Liu: Okay, we’ll pull that.

[11:34] Sara Kim: Another question: does tool calling change token count? We’re using function calls for JSON.

[11:40] Nadia Rahman: It can. The tool call arguments are tokens; also there can be system wrapper tokens for structured output. But again, it’s usually not a 14% delta unless most of your traffic is tool calls and you’re comparing against a different serialization.

[11:58] Priya Desai: It’s like 30% tool calls.

[12:01] Nadia Rahman: Not nothing, but still. We can include “tool_tokens” as a separate field if it helps. Internally we break it down.

[12:10] Mark Liu: That’d be nice.

[12:12] Jordan Blake: Great.

[12:15] (crosstalk)

[12:16] Jordan Blake: Sorry, go ahead Priya.

[12:18] Priya Desai: I was just going to say, we also do embeddings in batch. Different endpoint.

[12:24] Nadia Rahman: For embeddings, the meter is usually number of input tokens across all inputs. There was a known bug for batched embeddings where sometimes we under/over counted per input depending on batching. We’re patching it. But your delta is on chat, right?

[12:39] Mark Liu: Mostly chat completions, yes.

[12:42] Nadia Rahman: Okay.

[12:44] Jordan Blake: Let’s talk about dispute / remediation. We’ll do the analysis first. If it’s on us, we will correct it via credit or adjustment. But we don’t do retroactive invoice changes without Finance sign-off. So likely a credit memo.

[13:02] Mark Liu: That’s acceptable. We just need transparency.

[13:06] Sara Kim: And we need to know it won’t keep happening.

[13:09] Nadia Rahman: On that: we are rolling out stronger idempotency and a “shadow billing compare” mode. Which means we compute old and new meters in parallel for a period and verify deltas before it hits invoices.

[13:24] Priya Desai: When does that land.

[13:26] Nadia Rahman: We’re mid-rollout. Some changes are already in staging, prod early next week for your region. But I don’t want to promise a date without checking. Jordan and I can follow up with a timeline.

[13:39] Jordan Blake: We’ll send it.

[13:41] Mark Liu: Another thing: your plan says “billed tokens exclude cached prompt.” But our invoice has no mention of caching. Customers will ask like, are we being charged fairly.

[13:53] Jordan Blake: Totally. We’re updating our external FAQ and internal sales guidance. For now, what we can commit to is: billed tokens are what you pay for, raw tokens are what you used. And cache can reduce billed tokens when it’s hit.

[14:06] Sara Kim: I would prefer the inverse, like cache reduces cost, but only if you can measure it.

[14:10] Nadia Rahman: Agreed. That’s the point of adding those fields.

[14:15] Priya Desai: Quick technical: do you do any rounding? Like per-request minimum?

[14:21] Nadia Rahman: For chat/completions we don’t do per-request minimum tokens. For some legacy routes we had rounding to the nearest token block, but not for your plan.

[14:32] Mark Liu: Okay.

[14:34] Jordan Blake: I’m going to pause and see if there are other concerns we should capture before we go into next steps.

[14:41] Priya Desai: Just that our finance team needs a stable daily export going forward. Like a table we can pull.

[14:48] Nadia Rahman: Understood. Long-term you’ll be able to use the Usage API with billed_tokens and cache_hit and it should be consistent with ledger. Short-term we can do a scheduled export to your S3 or GCS, but we need to check with security/compliance.

[15:05] Mark Liu: Even a weekly CSV is fine to start.

[15:09] Jordan Blake: Okay.

[15:12] Nadia Rahman: Can I ask: are you using one account across prod and staging? Sometimes we see traffic from staging inflating usage.

[15:20] Priya Desai: No, different API keys. But same billing account. That might be a thing.

[15:26] Mark Liu: We would see that in our logs though.

[15:29] Nadia Rahman: In the export we can include “environment” if you tag keys. If not, at least key_id.

[15:37] Priya Desai: Please include key_id.

[15:40] Jordan Blake: Good catch.

[15:42] Nadia Rahman: Also region. Are you pinned to us-west or multi-region?

[15:47] Priya Desai: Mostly us-west, but we enabled eu-west for a subset.

[15:52] Nadia Rahman: Region mismatches were another known issue for some events. That could impact pricing if your EU rate differs.

[16:00] Mark Liu: Wait, EU is more expensive.

[16:03] Jordan Blake: Sometimes, depending on GPU cost. But we should verify that. If region was missing and defaulted, that could mess up totals.

[16:13] Nadia Rahman: Exactly. I’ll check whether any of your usage events are missing region dimension and got defaulted.

[16:22] Sara Kim: So potentially it’s not even tokens, it’s like, cost per token?

[16:26] Nadia Rahman: The discrepancy you quoted was tokens, but yes, region can affect cost. We’ll look at both.

[16:33] Jordan Blake: Okay.

[16:35] (brief silence)

[16:36] Jordan Blake: Let’s lock the deliverables.

[16:39] Jordan Blake: Nadia will send the meter definition in writing, and generate the export for Feb 1–5 with the fields we listed. Mark will send 2–3 request IDs from your side where you saw duplicate billing. Priya will confirm endpoint usage and idempotency behavior in your clients.

[16:56] Mark Liu: And when do we meet again.

[16:58] Jordan Blake: Can we do Tuesday 11am PT?

[17:02] Sara Kim: Works for me.

[17:03] Priya Desai: Yes.

[17:05] Mark Liu: Okay.

[17:07] Nadia Rahman: Great. Also, for transparency: if we find it’s a known streaming retry edge case, we’ll recommend either credit for affected period and confirm the fix is deployed, plus monitor with a sanity check alert.

[17:22] Mark Liu: Sounds good.

[17:24] Jordan Blake: Anything else before we wrap?

[17:27] Priya Desai: Just send the docs. We’ve been asked internally “what is a token” and we need consistent language.

[17:33] Jordan Blake: Yep. We’ll send a customer-facing explanation too. Thanks everyone.

[17:39] (call ends)

Note: Transcript is auto-generated. Speaker labels and technical terms may be imperfect (e.g., "KV cash" vs KV cache).

source.transcript:
Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
Recorded by Fireflies
Date: 2026-01-14
Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Dev Patel
Acme AI: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Miguel Santos, Hannah Wu

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed the Jan 10 Dedicated incident impacting Acme AI (elevated 5xx, latency, and intermittent 429s during a burst window).
- Redwood walked through timeline, mitigations (admission control changes, autoscale ceiling increase, capacity rebalance), and likely root cause (insufficient headroom + routing behavior under saturation).
- Customer feedback focused on earlier warning, clearer status updates, and what remediation SLAs mean for Dedicated.
- Agreed on follow-ups: share PIR, confirm classification, align on comms templates and headroom planning.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Incident timeline and impact
- Dedicated capacity saturation signals
- Router fallback / spillover behavior
- Client retry/backoff and streaming behavior
- Remediation SLAs and customer communications
- Preventative actions and monitoring

Questions (auto-generated)
- When does the mitigation clock start for Dedicated, and what counts as “mitigated”?
- Will Redwood page Acme on-call automatically for future Sev1/Sev0 incidents?
- Can we have a consistent comms template that doesn’t over-promise root cause?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the post-incident review for the January 10th event on your Dedicated cluster. Goal is to walk the timeline, what we did to mitigate, what we think the root cause was, and then spend time on your concerns around comms and SLAs. And we’ll leave with concrete next steps.

[00:18] Jordan Lee: Sounds good.

[00:19] Priya Nair: Yep.

[00:21] Rachel Kim: Quick intros for the recording—Rachel, I run enterprise customer comms on our side. Rafael is our SRE program lead, Lauren is product for Dedicated SLAs, and Dev is support escalation mapping.

[00:33] Miguel Santos: Miguel, ML infra.

[00:35] Hannah Wu: Hannah, I’m PM on our side.

[00:37] Priya Nair: Priya, SRE.

[00:39] Jordan Lee: Jordan, platform.

[00:41] Rachel Kim: Great. Rafael, do you want to do the timeline and symptoms?

[00:46] Rafael Mendes: Yep. So, incident start as we saw it: Jan 10, 18:07 UTC, alarms fired for elevated request error rate on your Dedicated endpoint—mostly 5xx at the gateway, plus a jump in p95 and p99 latency. The first strong indicator was GPU saturation in the us-east-1 pool allocated to your cluster and queue depth building at the scheduler.

[01:12] Priya Nair: Just to anchor it, for us it was around 10:10 Pacific. We saw timeouts and then some 429s.

[01:19] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. We saw the 429s shortly after. For the record, the 429s were admission control—so not like account quota—more like the system protecting itself. But the experience is the same.

[01:32] Jordan Lee: Right, it was mixed. Some calls were failing with 5xx and some were 429. And streaming sessions were dropping too.

[01:39] Rafael Mendes: We saw streaming disconnects, yes. Not the primary symptom, but correlated when the system got overloaded.

[01:46] Rachel Kim: And we’ll come back to streaming because I know it affects your UX.

[01:49] Rafael Mendes: Timeline: at 18:10 UTC on-call declared a Sev1. At 18:13 we started mitigation—first step was raising the autoscaling ceiling for your pool and shifting spare capacity from a lower-priority internal batch workload.

[02:08] Miguel Santos: Was that manual? Like you had to go in and bump it?

[02:11] Rafael Mendes: It was manual. Today Dedicated autoscale has guardrails; it won’t exceed a cap without approval, mostly to avoid surprise cost and also because it can starve neighboring pools. We bumped the cap and requested additional nodes.

[02:27] Priya Nair: And provisioning time was…?

[02:29] Rafael Mendes: That’s part of what hurt. New GPUs weren’t instant. We had ~11 to 14 minutes before we saw meaningful relief from new capacity. During that window, the router was trying to load-balance across replicas that were all near saturation.

[02:47] Jordan Lee: That was the rough part. We had a scheduled product push and the burst was planned.

[02:52] Rachel Kim: Yep, understood.

[02:54] Rafael Mendes: At 18:18 we also changed admission control behavior—moved it from “fail late” to “fail early” basically. That reduced tail latency, but it increased 429 rate for a bit. In effect it traded long timeouts for quicker rejects.

[03:12] Priya Nair: That actually helped us. Our retry logic handles 429 better than hanging sockets.

[03:18] Miguel Santos: For streaming though, quick reject is okay but mid-stream drop is worse.

[03:23] Rafael Mendes: Agree. We think the mid-stream drops were due to the gateway timing out upstream or the runtime worker being killed under memory pressure—there was some OOM risk. I’ll be careful: we don’t have 100% proof on the OOM path; the logs were noisy. But we saw “out of memory” in a couple of workers.

[03:45] Jordan Lee: Is that like KV cache pressure? We’ve seen that before.

[03:49] Rafael Mendes: Yes—KV cache pressure, sequence length skew. (Transcription note: he said “K V”, not “key cash”, but yeah.) The burst had longer prompts than your baseline.

[04:03] Priya Nair: That’s true, we had a new feature that adds retrieval context.

[04:07] Rafael Mendes: Right. So, at 18:24 UTC we got additional capacity online, errors started dropping. By 18:31 UTC we were back within SLO for error rate, and by 18:36 tail latency was back to normal-ish. We kept monitoring until 19:05 and closed the incident at 19:12.

[04:28] Rachel Kim: So total customer-visible impact window—about 29 minutes for elevated errors/latency, with the sharpest part in the first ~15.

[04:37] Hannah Wu: That matches our notes.

[04:39] Rachel Kim: Can we talk classification? This is something we’re standardizing. Rafael, what category are we putting this under?

[04:46] Rafael Mendes: For our incident taxonomy v1, the primary incident type is “Capacity / Fleet shortage” and the subtype would be “Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag.” We also had contributing factors from routing—specifically “Routing/fallback behavior under overload” but we would not make routing the primary owner here.

[05:07] Jordan Lee: That’s the thing. From our perspective it felt like routing made it worse. Like it thrashed.

[05:14] Rafael Mendes: Totally fair. Let me describe what we saw. The router used health and latency to pick replicas. Under saturation, latency got weird, so it oscillated more than we’d like. But the initiating condition was simply not enough headroom for the prompt distribution in that burst.

Four: add a runbook + internal checklist so the on-call knows the tradeoff between failing fast (429) vs tail latency.

[14:40] Priya Nair: And streaming disconnects? Any specific fix?

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: We suspect some disconnects were gateway timeouts. We can tune the upstream idle timeout for streaming in your Dedicated gateway and ensure we’re not killing worker processes under memory pressure. I don’t want to promise full elimination without validating. But we’ll investigate.

[15:05] Miguel Santos: Our SDK upgrade around that time—could that have contributed?

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:13] Rafael Mendes: Possibly, but the correlated saturation signals make me think server-side overload was primary. Still, if the new SDK changed retry behavior, it could amplify load. If you can share retry/backoff settings, we’ll sanity check.

[15:31] Jordan Lee: We can send that.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:35] Hannah Wu: On the SLA / remediation language—can we get something we can copy/paste internally? Like definition of Sev1, mitigation, etc.

[15:44] Lauren Bishop: Yes. I’ll send the one-pager. It basically says: Severity is based on customer impact; mitigation target depends on tier; Dedicated has tighter comms expectations; exceptions include cloud provider outages, but we try to mitigate regardless.

[16:03] Jordan Lee: We’d like to know what counts as “cloud provider outage.” Because that can feel like a loophole.

[16:10] Lauren Bishop: Totally. It’s not meant as a loophole. It’s mainly for credit calculations, not for our operational urgency. Operationally, we still mitigate. The carve-out is for when the underlying cloud region is broadly impaired and we can’t provision/route around it.

[16:27] Priya Nair: In this case, it wasn’t that.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[16:31] Rachel Kim: Can we talk about escalation path? During the incident, you posted in shared Slack. Did you know who to ping?

[16:38] Priya Nair: We pinged our TAM alias and then ended up in the incident channel. It worked, but it was a little chaotic.

[16:45] Dev Patel: We’re standardizing: for Dedicated Sev1/Sev0, you’ll get a single comms thread in shared Slack with an owner, and a backup email summary. And the incident commander role on our side is clear.

[17:00] Jordan Lee: That helps.

[17:02] Miguel Santos: Also, if the mitigation is “more 429s,” we want to know that explicitly so we can adjust client behavior.

[17:09] Rafael Mendes: Great call. We’ll include “expected client behavior” in comms: whether to retry, backoff, or reduce concurrency.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:21] Priya Nair: One more technical: did you do any region failover?

[17:25] Rafael Mendes: No region failover. For your Dedicated, you’re single-region today with warm spares, but not active-active. We did not move to another region.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

text:
Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
Recorded by Fireflies
Date: 2026-01-14
Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Dev Patel
Acme AI: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Miguel Santos, Hannah Wu

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed the Jan 10 Dedicated incident impacting Acme AI (elevated 5xx, latency, and intermittent 429s during a burst window).
- Redwood walked through timeline, mitigations (admission control changes, autoscale ceiling increase, capacity rebalance), and likely root cause (insufficient headroom + routing behavior under saturation).
- Customer feedback focused on earlier warning, clearer status updates, and what remediation SLAs mean for Dedicated.
- Agreed on follow-ups: share PIR, confirm classification, align on comms templates and headroom planning.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Incident timeline and impact
- Dedicated capacity saturation signals
- Router fallback / spillover behavior
- Client retry/backoff and streaming behavior
- Remediation SLAs and customer communications
- Preventative actions and monitoring

Questions (auto-generated)
- When does the mitigation clock start for Dedicated, and what counts as “mitigated”?
- Will Redwood page Acme on-call automatically for future Sev1/Sev0 incidents?
- Can we have a consistent comms template that doesn’t over-promise root cause?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the post-incident review for the January 10th event on your Dedicated cluster. Goal is to walk the timeline, what we did to mitigate, what we think the root cause was, and then spend time on your concerns around comms and SLAs. And we’ll leave with concrete next steps.

[00:18] Jordan Lee: Sounds good.

[00:19] Priya Nair: Yep.

[00:21] Rachel Kim: Quick intros for the recording—Rachel, I run enterprise customer comms on our side. Rafael is our SRE program lead, Lauren is product for Dedicated SLAs, and Dev is support escalation mapping.

[00:33] Miguel Santos: Miguel, ML infra.

[00:35] Hannah Wu: Hannah, I’m PM on our side.

[00:37] Priya Nair: Priya, SRE.

[00:39] Jordan Lee: Jordan, platform.

[00:41] Rachel Kim: Great. Rafael, do you want to do the timeline and symptoms?

[00:46] Rafael Mendes: Yep. So, incident start as we saw it: Jan 10, 18:07 UTC, alarms fired for elevated request error rate on your Dedicated endpoint—mostly 5xx at the gateway, plus a jump in p95 and p99 latency. The first strong indicator was GPU saturation in the us-east-1 pool allocated to your cluster and queue depth building at the scheduler.

[01:12] Priya Nair: Just to anchor it, for us it was around 10:10 Pacific. We saw timeouts and then some 429s.

[01:19] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. We saw the 429s shortly after. For the record, the 429s were admission control—so not like account quota—more like the system protecting itself. But the experience is the same.

[01:32] Jordan Lee: Right, it was mixed. Some calls were failing with 5xx and some were 429. And streaming sessions were dropping too.

[01:39] Rafael Mendes: We saw streaming disconnects, yes. Not the primary symptom, but correlated when the system got overloaded.

[01:46] Rachel Kim: And we’ll come back to streaming because I know it affects your UX.

[01:49] Rafael Mendes: Timeline: at 18:10 UTC on-call declared a Sev1. At 18:13 we started mitigation—first step was raising the autoscaling ceiling for your pool and shifting spare capacity from a lower-priority internal batch workload.

[02:08] Miguel Santos: Was that manual? Like you had to go in and bump it?

[02:11] Rafael Mendes: It was manual. Today Dedicated autoscale has guardrails; it won’t exceed a cap without approval, mostly to avoid surprise cost and also because it can starve neighboring pools. We bumped the cap and requested additional nodes.

[02:27] Priya Nair: And provisioning time was…?

[02:29] Rafael Mendes: That’s part of what hurt. New GPUs weren’t instant. We had ~11 to 14 minutes before we saw meaningful relief from new capacity. During that window, the router was trying to load-balance across replicas that were all near saturation.

[02:47] Jordan Lee: That was the rough part. We had a scheduled product push and the burst was planned.

[02:52] Rachel Kim: Yep, understood.

[02:54] Rafael Mendes: At 18:18 we also changed admission control behavior—moved it from “fail late” to “fail early” basically. That reduced tail latency, but it increased 429 rate for a bit. In effect it traded long timeouts for quicker rejects.

[03:12] Priya Nair: That actually helped us. Our retry logic handles 429 better than hanging sockets.

[03:18] Miguel Santos: For streaming though, quick reject is okay but mid-stream drop is worse.

[03:23] Rafael Mendes: Agree. We think the mid-stream drops were due to the gateway timing out upstream or the runtime worker being killed under memory pressure—there was some OOM risk. I’ll be careful: we don’t have 100% proof on the OOM path; the logs were noisy. But we saw “out of memory” in a couple of workers.

[03:45] Jordan Lee: Is that like KV cache pressure? We’ve seen that before.

[03:49] Rafael Mendes: Yes—KV cache pressure, sequence length skew. (Transcription note: he said “K V”, not “key cash”, but yeah.) The burst had longer prompts than your baseline.

[04:03] Priya Nair: That’s true, we had a new feature that adds retrieval context.

[04:07] Rafael Mendes: Right. So, at 18:24 UTC we got additional capacity online, errors started dropping. By 18:31 UTC we were back within SLO for error rate, and by 18:36 tail latency was back to normal-ish. We kept monitoring until 19:05 and closed the incident at 19:12.

[04:28] Rachel Kim: So total customer-visible impact window—about 29 minutes for elevated errors/latency, with the sharpest part in the first ~15.

[04:37] Hannah Wu: That matches our notes.

[04:39] Rachel Kim: Can we talk classification? This is something we’re standardizing. Rafael, what category are we putting this under?

[04:46] Rafael Mendes: For our incident taxonomy v1, the primary incident type is “Capacity / Fleet shortage” and the subtype would be “Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag.” We also had contributing factors from routing—specifically “Routing/fallback behavior under overload” but we would not make routing the primary owner here.

[05:07] Jordan Lee: That’s the thing. From our perspective it felt like routing made it worse. Like it thrashed.

[05:14] Rafael Mendes: Totally fair. Let me describe what we saw. The router used health and latency to pick replicas. Under saturation, latency got weird, so it oscillated more than we’d like. But the initiating condition was simply not enough headroom for the prompt distribution in that burst.

[05:33] Priya Nair: So primary owner is your fleet team?

[05:36] Rafael Mendes: Primary owner would be Infra/Fleet for the capacity piece. Routing team is consulted on the oscillation issue.

[05:44] Rachel Kim: And for you, it changes who gets paged first and who writes the main corrective actions.

[05:49] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[05:51] Lauren Bishop: If I can jump in, from a Dedicated commitments standpoint, classification matters because we report incident volume and remediation SLA compliance by type and owner. But for you, what matters is: how quickly we mitigate, how we communicate, and what we do to prevent repeats.

[06:06] Miguel Santos: Right. Can you define “mitigate”? Because you said 18:31 we were back within SLO, but we still saw some elevated latency after.

[06:15] Lauren Bishop: Great question. The draft policy we’re rolling out—still in final approval—defines time-to-mitigate as “customer impact materially reduced and stable,” typically meaning error rate and p95 are within agreed bounds for a sustained period, not just a single datapoint. For Dedicated, we’re being explicit: mitigated is not “we found the issue,” it’s “your endpoint is serving within the operational envelope.”

[06:42] Priya Nair: And time-to-fix is like root cause fix shipped?

[06:45] Lauren Bishop: Exactly. Fix is the durable change—config, code, capacity planning, alerts—whatever prevents recurrence. That’s usually days, not minutes.

[06:56] Jordan Lee: So on Jan 10, would you call mitigation at 18:31?

[07:01] Rafael Mendes: We would tentatively call it mitigated at ~18:36 when latency stabilized. Errors were down earlier but tail was still bouncing.

[07:10] Rachel Kim: And we’ll put that explicitly in the write-up so it’s not ambiguous.

[07:14] Miguel Santos: Okay.

[07:16] Rachel Kim: Customer concerns—Jordan, you had a few bullets in email. Want to walk through them?

[07:22] Jordan Lee: Yeah. One is proactive notice. We only got a heads up after we’d already paged internally. Two is clarity: we got “elevated errors, investigating,” which is fine, but then later we got a message that implied it was “region instability” which spooked our leadership. Three is: do we get credits, and how do the SLAs apply to Dedicated.

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yep, all fair.

[07:50] Dev Patel: On comms, we’re trying to standardize templates by incident type so we avoid over-specific claims mid-incident. Like “capacity saturation in your Dedicated pool, mitigations in progress, next update in X minutes.” Instead of guessing root cause.

[08:07] Priya Nair: Please do. The “region instability” thing made it sound like AWS was on fire.

[08:12] Rafael Mendes: That was on us. Internally we were looking at regional capacity constraints and someone used that phrasing. It wasn’t a full region outage.

[08:22] Rachel Kim: For proactive notice: today, our incident-bot posts to your shared Slack channel once we declare, but we didn’t declare until we got enough signal. That’s too slow for Dedicated customers with tight windows. A thing we can do is add a “pre-incident advisory” when we see sustained saturation risk.

[08:42] Jordan Lee: Like, “we see you ramping, we’re watching.”

[08:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly.

[08:46] Miguel Santos: Could you page us directly? We have PagerDuty.

[08:49] Rachel Kim: We can talk about options. Some customers prefer Slack + email. Direct PD paging is possible but we need to coordinate severity thresholds so we don’t page you for every blip.

[09:03] Hannah Wu: We’d want it only for Sev1 and Sev0, I think.

[09:06] Lauren Bishop: That aligns with our thinking.

[09:09] Priya Nair: Another question: were we spilling over to Hosted? Because we saw some response differences. Slight quality drift.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Important. For your Dedicated, your policy is “pinned model version” and “no spillover to hosted” by default. During the incident we did NOT enable spillover to hosted. What may have happened is that the router selected a different replica group running the same model but with a slightly different quantization profile. That’s on us; it should be consistent.

[09:41] Miguel Santos: That’s a big deal for us. We A/B on our side and any drift causes false positives.

[09:47] Rafael Mendes: Understood. We have an internal control plane setting to prevent profile variance across the pool; we’re going to enforce that for your cluster.

[09:57] Jordan Lee: So we can say there was no model change, but there may have been a different “profile”?

[10:02] Rafael Mendes: Correct. Same model version. Different execution profile. We’ll write it that way.

[10:10] Rachel Kim: And in customer comms we’ll avoid saying “fallback model” unless it truly was.

[10:15] Priya Nair: Good.

[10:16] Rachel Kim: Lauren, can you cover credits / SLA?

[10:20] Lauren Bishop: Yep. For Dedicated, your contract has an uptime commitment and a support response commitment. The remediation SLAs we’re rolling out are operational targets—time to mitigate/fix—used to drive internal behavior and reporting. They’re not automatically the same as contractual credits, but they should map sensibly.

[10:41] Jordan Lee: So are we eligible for credits from this event?

[10:44] Lauren Bishop: We’ll evaluate it against the SLA definition in your order form: downtime minutes and measurement method. This incident looks like partial unavailability rather than full outage; we need to quantify. Rachel and Dev will follow up with the calculation approach we use.

[11:00] Dev Patel: And we can do a post-incident letter summarizing impact and mitigations. Some customers use that for internal reporting even if credits aren’t triggered.

[11:09] Hannah Wu: That would help.

[11:11] Miguel Santos: Technical question: why didn’t autoscale kick in earlier? We had a predictable ramp.

[11:17] Rafael Mendes: Two reasons. One: the autoscaler keys off a few signals—GPU utilization, queue depth, and request rate. The request rate ramped, but the queue depth didn’t cross threshold until prompts got longer. Two: we cap autoscale for Dedicated to avoid runaway. Your cap was set based on your previous peak. You exceeded it.

[11:40] Priya Nair: So we should rebaseline the cap.

[11:43] Rafael Mendes: Yes. And we can set a “burst window” schedule that temporarily increases headroom during your known peak. That’s something Fleet can do.

[11:53] Jordan Lee: That’d be ideal. Our peak is 9 to 11 Pacific weekdays.

[11:57] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[12:00] Miguel Santos: Also, you mentioned OOM. Does that mean we need a max tokens guardrail?

[12:05] Rafael Mendes: Potentially. You’re already at max_new_tokens=512, but total context length increased. We can add a safeguard at the gateway to reject extreme prompt sizes with a clear error before it harms the cluster.

[12:20] Priya Nair: We’d prefer a 4xx with “prompt too large” rather than random 5xx.

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[12:26] Rachel Kim: On the comms side, that also gives us cleaner language: “requests exceeded configured limits.”

[12:33] Jordan Lee: Another comms point: update cadence. We got updates at like minute 0 and then minute 25.

[12:41] Rachel Kim: Yeah, that’s not acceptable for Sev1. Our draft template is every 10–15 minutes during active mitigation, even if the update is “still mitigating, no ETA.” We’ll standardize that.

[12:54] Dev Patel: And we’ll keep a “what we know / what we don’t know” section so we don’t speculate.

[13:01] Priya Nair: +1.

[13:03] Miguel Santos: Can you share the metrics you used? We only see the client side.

[13:07] Rafael Mendes: We can share a redacted snapshot: saturation, queue depth, error codes breakdown. For Dedicated, we can also expose some of this in Console, but it’s ops-only right now.

[13:21] Rachel Kim: We’re working on what’s safe to expose to CS and to customers; right now we’ll do a manual share for this incident.

[13:29] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[13:31] Rafael Mendes: I also want to address the routing thrash comment. We have a workstream to instrument “fallback reasons” and routing decisions better, so we can distinguish capacity-driven routing from policy misconfig.

[13:45] Priya Nair: Yeah, because from the outside it’s just errors.

[13:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Today it’s too ambiguous.

[13:51] Lauren Bishop: This ties to remediation SLAs too. If it’s a pure fleet shortage, fix path is headroom + provisioning. If it’s routing misbehavior, fix path is code/config. Different owners.

[14:04] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk preventative actions. Rafael, what are you committing to on Redwood side?

[14:11] Rafael Mendes: Four items. One: adjust your Dedicated autoscale cap and add scheduled burst headroom. Two: enforce consistent execution profile across your pool—no silent drift. Three: implement earlier warning alerts—when we detect approaching saturation for Dedicated, we proactively notify you in Slack. Four: add a runbook + internal checklist so the on-call knows the tradeoff between failing fast (429) vs tail latency.

[14:40] Priya Nair: And streaming disconnects? Any specific fix?

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: We suspect some disconnects were gateway timeouts. We can tune the upstream idle timeout for streaming in your Dedicated gateway and ensure we’re not killing worker processes under memory pressure. I don’t want to promise full elimination without validating. But we’ll investigate.

[15:05] Miguel Santos: Our SDK upgrade around that time—could that have contributed?

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:13] Rafael Mendes: Possibly, but the correlated saturation signals make me think server-side overload was primary. Still, if the new SDK changed retry behavior, it could amplify load. If you can share retry/backoff settings, we’ll sanity check.

[15:31] Jordan Lee: We can send that.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:35] Hannah Wu: On the SLA / remediation language—can we get something we can copy/paste internally? Like definition of Sev1, mitigation, etc.

[15:44] Lauren Bishop: Yes. I’ll send the one-pager. It basically says: Severity is based on customer impact; mitigation target depends on tier; Dedicated has tighter comms expectations; exceptions include cloud provider outages, but we try to mitigate regardless.

[16:03] Jordan Lee: We’d like to know what counts as “cloud provider outage.” Because that can feel like a loophole.

[16:10] Lauren Bishop: Totally. It’s not meant as a loophole. It’s mainly for credit calculations, not for our operational urgency. Operationally, we still mitigate. The carve-out is for when the underlying cloud region is broadly impaired and we can’t provision/route around it.

[16:27] Priya Nair: In this case, it wasn’t that.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[16:31] Rachel Kim: Can we talk about escalation path? During the incident, you posted in shared Slack. Did you know who to ping?

[16:38] Priya Nair: We pinged our TAM alias and then ended up in the incident channel. It worked, but it was a little chaotic.

[16:45] Dev Patel: We’re standardizing: for Dedicated Sev1/Sev0, you’ll get a single comms thread in shared Slack with an owner, and a backup email summary. And the incident commander role on our side is clear.

[17:00] Jordan Lee: That helps.

[17:02] Miguel Santos: Also, if the mitigation is “more 429s,” we want to know that explicitly so we can adjust client behavior.

[17:09] Rafael Mendes: Great call. We’ll include “expected client behavior” in comms: whether to retry, backoff, or reduce concurrency.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:21] Priya Nair: One more technical: did you do any region failover?

[17:25] Rafael Mendes: No region failover. For your Dedicated, you’re single-region today with warm spares, but not active-active. We did not move to another region.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

[19:05] Jordan Lee: Works.

[19:06] Priya Nair: Thanks.

source.title: Acme AI - incident review + diagnostics gaps (deploy/config/saturation)
source.summary: Acme AI walked Redwood through the 3/10 incident impact (p95 latency spike + intermittent 503s on a subset of routes) and the biggest diagnostic gaps: (1) no easy “what deployed recently” context tied to the incident thread, (2) config/routing changes were hard to confirm quickly and safely (feature flags, model pins, quota/rate-limit) without manually paging multiple teams, and (3) saturation signals were inconsistent (GPU util looked fine, but queue depth + KV cache pressure were elevated). Redwood shared planned Incident Bot v2 context cards (recent deploys, tenant-safe config diffs with redaction, saturation signals + deep links), plus constraints around permissioning/audit logging and query budgets. Agreed on a short beta for Acme’s on-call runbooks and which dashboards/links would be most useful.
topics: Incident recap: symptoms, blast radius, and timeline gaps
Deploy context: service/version, canary vs full rollout, links to PR/commit
Config diff needs: feature flags, routing policy, quota changes, model pins; redaction + audit
Saturation signals: GPU util vs queue depth vs KV cache pressure; regional capacity and backpressure
Deep links: console dashboards, traces/log correlation, error budget burn views
Beta/rollout plan for Incident Bot v2 and success criteria (time-to-triage, completeness)

Jacob Stein - Share sample Incident Bot v2 context card screenshots (deploy/config/sat) and get feedback on what to prioritize (by 2025-03-14)

Elaine Chen (Acme) - Provide Acme’s security requirements for audit log retention + who can access config diffs during incidents (by 2025-03-19)
Miguel Alvarez (Acme) - Share their current “first 5 minutes” checklist/runbook so Redwood can map bot cards to it (by 2025-03-17)

Title: Acme AI - incident review + diagnostics gaps (deploy/config/saturation)
Date: 2025-03-12
Time: 5:03 PM - 5:57 PM (PT)
Duration: 54 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Dev Patel, Owen Phillips, Miles Thornton, Jacob Stein, Logan Wright
Acme AI: Sarah Kim, Miguel Alvarez, Anika Rao, Jordan Price, Elaine Chen

Auto-summary (generated)
- Acme described the incident as “slow then spiky”: p95 latency rose first, then bursts of 503s.

[00:00] Dev Patel: Okay, cool. Folks are in. Thanks everyone for making time. This is Dev from Redwood, I run the support escalation side. Goal today is… not to relive the pain too much, but to understand what was missing for you in the first like five, ten minutes.

[00:18] Sarah Kim: Yeah. Appreciate you all jumping on. For context, this was Monday for us, 3/10. We had like elevated p95 and then some intermittent 503s on our chat completion route.

[00:29] Owen Phillips: Owen here, I’m CS on the account. I’ll mostly listen, but yeah, we want to make sure we fix the “day two ops” stuff, not just this one incident.

[00:39] Miguel Alvarez: Miguel, I’m the SRE on call that day. I’ll be blunt—our first 20 minutes were basically Slack archeology.

[00:48] (crosstalk)

[00:50] Dev Patel: That’s fair. Maybe we start with a quick timeline from your side, Miguel, then we’ll drill into the diagnostics gaps. Sound good?

[01:00] Miguel Alvarez: Yep. So around 10:12 AM Pacific we got a latency page. Not like total outage, but p95 went from ~650ms to like 1.8 seconds on one of the primary routes. Then at 10:18-ish we start seeing 503s, but only in bursts.

[01:18] Miguel Alvarez: Our first question was, “did Redwood deploy something,” because the shape looked like a regression. We asked in the shared channel, but we didn’t have anything in the incident thread that said “here are recent deploys for serving-api in us-west” or whatever.

[01:34] Sarah Kim: And separately, we had made a routing policy tweak earlier that morning. Minor. But we couldn’t quickly confirm whether it had fully propagated or whether it overlapped with Redwood changes.

[01:49] Jacob Stein: Jacob, I’m on diagnostics tooling. When you say routing policy tweak, is that like model pin, or traffic split, or…?

[01:56] Sarah Kim: For us it was a traffic split between two model variants. We were trying to roll back a quant profile—sorry, I don’t remember your exact term. We basically adjusted the fallback chain.

[02:10] Jacob Stein: Got it. That’s exactly the kind of thing we want to surface safely.

[02:15] Dev Patel: And then saturation—did you suspect capacity right away, or did it look more like errors?

[02:22] Miguel Alvarez: Initially we thought saturation, because latency rose first. But the GPU utilization dashboard we have access to looked… fine? Like 60-65%. Which was confusing.

[02:33] Anika Rao: Yeah, and we were looking at tokens per second and it dropped, but not in a way that screamed “no capacity.” More like queueing.

[02:43] Logan Wright: Logan from telemetry. That “GPU util looks fine but everything is slow” is classic queue depth / KV cache pressure territory. Depending on batching/backpressure, you can have headroom on raw GPU while the scheduler is choking.

[03:00] Jordan Price: Jordan, backend. The other issue was we had to open like three different tools. One dashboard for GPU, another for request errors, and then we were asking your team for “what changed.” It’s just not one workflow.

[03:16] Dev Patel: Yep. So that’s basically the project we’re doing internally—Incident Bot v2—push the “what changed” plus “are we saturated” into the incident thread automatically.

[03:27] Elaine Chen: Before we go too far, Elaine from security. When you say “push into the thread,” does that include tenant configuration? Because that’s sensitive on our side.

[03:36] Miles Thornton: Miles here, I own incident automation. Great question. The plan is a tenant-safe config diff: allowlist keys, redact anything that looks like a secret, and emit audit log events any time the bot fetches it. We can also restrict who can expand details.

[03:55] Elaine Chen: Okay. We’ll need to understand retention on the audit logs and who the “break glass” approver is.

[04:02] Dev Patel: Totally. We’ll come back to that. Miguel, can you describe the moment you felt blocked?

[04:10] Miguel Alvarez: Honestly right after the initial page. We posted “any deploys?” and we waited. Meanwhile we’re trying to decide: do we fail over regions? do we reduce max tokens? We didn’t know if we were in a canary or full rollout.

[04:28] Miguel Alvarez: If we had a card that said “Serving API deployed 17 minutes ago, phase: 20% canary, canary error rate elevated,” we would’ve immediately frozen changes and focused on rollback.

[04:44] Jacob Stein: That’s helpful. So the deploy card needs: service, version/SHA, rollout phase, and canary health summary. Plus links to PR/commit.

[04:55] Sarah Kim: Yeah, and ideally scope it to the region. Because “a deploy happened somewhere” isn’t useful.

[05:03] Logan Wright: Region scoping is doable as long as we can map deploy events to the serving pools. That’s work we’re mid-flight on.

[05:13] Dev Patel: On the config side, what would have saved you time?

[05:18] Sarah Kim: A diff. Like, “routing policy changed at 09:47 by Sarah, changed fallback order A->B to A->C” and “quota changed” kind of thing.

[05:30] Sarah Kim: We had to go into our internal change log plus your console plus ask support. And we still weren’t sure if it propagated.

[05:40] Jordan Price: And the feature flags. I don’t know if you call them flags or toggles, but we needed to know if something flipped.

[05:47] Miles Thornton: Internally we call them feature flags and policy toggles. The planned config diff card includes: feature flags, routing policy, quota/rate limit, and model pins. The key is redaction: we don’t want to leak prompts, customer IDs, anything like that.

[06:05] Elaine Chen: Also, we don’t want config diffs dumped into a big shared Slack where not all participants are authorized.

[06:12] Owen Phillips: Understood. For most customers we keep incident channels limited, but we can also have the bot post a summary and require an interaction to view more.

[06:23] Jacob Stein: Like “Diff available: 12 changes, 4 redacted,” then click to open a modal if you have permission.

[06:31] Elaine Chen: That’s the right pattern.

[06:34] Dev Patel: Switching to saturation signals—what did you wish you saw beyond GPU util?

[06:41] Miguel Alvarez: Queue depth per region. Something that says “requests are stacking up.”

[06:47] Anika Rao: KV cache pressure, if you have it. We saw longer sequences during that window, and I suspect it blew out cache.

[06:55] Logan Wright: We do have KV cache metrics, but sometimes the label cardinality explodes if we do it wrong. We’re standardizing it. The bot will show a summary: “KV cache pressure high” with a link to the dashboard rather than raw per-model lines.

[07:13] Jordan Price: And ideally also “scheduler backpressure.” We kept hearing that term from your support during the incident, but we couldn’t see it.

[07:22] Logan Wright: Yep. We call it admission control / backpressure indicators. The bot card for saturation is planned to include: GPU utilization, queue depth, scheduler saturation, and KV cache pressure. Plus a quick “likely cause” hint, but we’ll be careful not to over-automate guesses.

[07:42] Dev Patel: Let’s talk about the deep links. During the incident, what did you click most?

[07:48] Miguel Alvarez: Traces. But we didn’t have the trace link that was already scoped to the time window.

[07:55] Sarah Kim: We also wanted a “dashboard bundle” link. One click to open the right service, right region, last 30 minutes.

[08:05] Miles Thornton: That’s in scope: links into Console dashboards and trace/log correlation links. The trick is making sure it lands correctly and doesn’t time out.

[08:17] Jacob Stein: Also, if upstream telemetry is slow during an incident, the bot should degrade to cached context + links rather than hanging.

[08:27] Miguel Alvarez: That would be huge. We had your dashboards go sluggish while we were trying to debug, which is kind of ironic.

[08:36] Logan Wright: Yeah, that’s a known failure mode. We’re adding caching and query budgets so the bot doesn’t DoS the metrics system.

[08:46] Dev Patel: For the record, during Monday we also saw a telemetry query latency spike. That’s on us.

[08:53] Sarah Kim: Understood. We just want predictable “first five minutes” data.

[09:00] Dev Patel: Okay. I’m going to sanity-check what I heard:
- Card 1: Recent deploys with canary health, per service/region, link to PR.
- Card 2: Config diff with redaction, propagation hints, and audit.
- Card 3: Saturation signals beyond GPU util: queue depth, KV pressure, scheduler/backpressure.
Plus deep links and degraded mode.

[09:23] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[09:24] Miguel Alvarez: And one more—some indication of “regional capacity constraints.” Like, is us-east out of headroom? Because we were debating failover.

[09:34] Logan Wright: That’s part of the regional capacity signal. We can show “capacity tight” per pool, but again, summarized.

[09:43] Anika Rao: Also, if you show that, it should include whether fallback routing is active. Because our traffic might be shifted without us realizing.

[09:53] Jacob Stein: That ties into routing policy context. We can include “fallback engaged” and “model variant” changes.

[10:04] Elaine Chen: Quick question on the config diff: will it show the actor? Like, who changed it?

[10:09] Miles Thornton: Where it’s our control plane changes, yes, we can show “changed by” if it’s a Redwood operator or an automated rollout. For customer-initiated changes, we can show the principal if your identity is federated. If not, it may be an API key name.

[10:26] Elaine Chen: Okay. Just make sure it doesn’t expose secrets. API key names are fine.

[10:31] Dev Patel: On the incident itself—do you want to walk through the root cause we found, or keep this focused on diagnostics needs?

[10:39] Sarah Kim: We can do five minutes on cause, but the bigger value is the tooling. We already got the post-incident note.

[10:47] Dev Patel: Got it. Very quick: there was a deploy affecting request scheduling logic in one region. It increased queueing under certain sequence length distributions. That’s why GPU didn’t look pegged.

[11:05] Miguel Alvarez: That matches what we saw. Okay.

[11:08] Jordan Price: So that deploy card would have basically told us “scheduling changed,” yeah?

[11:13] Jacob Stein: It would at least tell you “a deploy happened.” We’re also exploring attaching “related PRs” and a short change summary, but we need to make sure it’s accurate.

[11:27] Owen Phillips: And to be clear, this is not just for Redwood-internal incidents. We want it to help your on-call too.

[11:35] Sarah Kim: Right. Our on-call needs to know: do we roll back our routing change, or is Redwood rolling something back.

[11:44] Dev Patel: On the beta: we’re planning a feature-flagged rollout. Would Acme be open to being a design partner? Basically you’d see the cards in your incident threads and we’d iterate.

[11:57] Sarah Kim: Yes, with two caveats: security sign-off on config diff access, and we don’t want additional noise.

[12:06] Miguel Alvarez: And it can’t slow down response. If the bot posts 30 seconds later than the page, it’s still useful, but if it’s like 10 minutes later, no.

[12:16] Miles Thornton: Understood. We’re targeting sub-60 seconds for the first post, with cached follow-ups.

[12:25] Logan Wright: And we’ll cap query cost. Under high load it will fall back to links.

[12:33] Elaine Chen: How do you handle audit logs? Like, if someone expands a config diff, does that get logged?

[12:40] Miles Thornton: Yes. “Bot accessed config diff” plus “user requested expanded view” are separate audit events. They go to our SIEM pipeline. We can share retention defaults and options.

[12:55] Elaine Chen: We’ll want at least 1 year retention.

[12:58] Owen Phillips: Noted.

[13:01] Dev Patel: Let’s go deeper on what “config diff” means for you. Sarah, what objects would you want included?

[13:08] Sarah Kim: Routing policy, model pins, rate limits, and any rollout toggles. If you can also show “last updated timestamp” that would be great.

[13:20] Jordan Price: And it should show if the change partially applied. We’ve had cases where a policy is updated but only in one region.

[13:28] Jacob Stein: That’s the “partial propagation” problem. We can include a propagation status if the control plane has it. Otherwise we can at least show “observed config version” per region.

[13:45] Logan Wright: From telemetry, we can sometimes infer it by seeing the policy version label—again, careful about cardinality.

[13:54] Miguel Alvarez: Even a hint would help. Our team spent time arguing whether the change “took.”

[14:03] Dev Patel: On saturation thresholds—what would you want the bot to highlight? Like, should it say “red” at queue depth > X?

[14:12] Miguel Alvarez: We’d like “this is unusual compared to baseline,” not just a fixed number. But we’ll take fixed if that’s all.

[14:23] Logan Wright: We can do both. For the card, likely: fixed threshold + delta vs 7-day baseline. But we have to keep it simple.

[14:34] Anika Rao: For KV cache pressure, can you translate it into “you’re evicting” or “cache hit rate dropped”?

[14:40] Logan Wright: Yeah, we track eviction rate and hit ratio. The bot can say “KV evictions elevated” which is more actionable.

[14:51] Jordan Price: Actionable means “reduce max tokens” or “change batching,” right?

[14:56] Jacob Stein: Exactly, and the bot can link to the runbook. We’re updating runbooks so responders don’t guess.

[15:06] Miguel Alvarez: If the bot said “KV evictions high; mitigation: reduce context length or lower concurrency,” that’s already helpful.

[15:16] Miles Thornton: We’ll probably keep the card to: signal + link to runbook, to avoid bad automation. But we can include a short “common mitigations” snippet.

[15:28] Dev Patel: Let’s touch on deep links again. Jordan, what did you want as a single click?

[15:34] Jordan Price: A link that opens the dashboard with the right filters. We kept losing time typing region/service filters.

[15:44] Owen Phillips: Like a permalink with service=acme-chat, region=us-west2, time=last_30m.

[15:50] Sarah Kim: Exactly.

[15:52] Logan Wright: Also for traces, we can embed a pre-filtered trace explorer link. There’s some auth constraints but doable.

[16:02] Elaine Chen: As long as it respects RBAC.

[16:05] Logan Wright: Yes, it goes through the same auth.

[16:10] Dev Patel: I want to capture one more pain point: during Monday, did you feel you had to ask Redwood engineers for basic facts?

[16:18] Miguel Alvarez: Yes. We asked “is there a deploy” and “what’s the current canary” and “is there capacity.” That’s not a criticism, but it’s a lot of back-and-forth.

[16:31] Sarah Kim: And it’s not scalable if we’re going to run more workloads on you.

[16:36] Dev Patel: Agreed.

[16:38] Jacob Stein: One design question: would you prefer one big post or multiple cards?

[16:44] Miguel Alvarez: Multiple cards. Big posts get ignored.

[16:49] Sarah Kim: Yeah, three cards max, then drill-down.

[16:53] Miles Thornton: That matches our plan: Deploy / Config / Saturation. Plus a small “links” footer.

[17:02] Anika Rao: And maybe “error budget burn” if you have it.

[17:06] Dev Patel: We do have an error budget view internally. For customers, we can at least show the SLO burn rate chart link. We’ll see what we can expose.

[17:17] Owen Phillips: We’ve been discussing a “SLO toolkit” link generator. That’s coming.

[17:23] Sarah Kim: Good.

[17:25] Elaine Chen: Can we talk about who can see config diffs? On our side, we’d like only platform on-call and security, not every developer.

[17:35] Miles Thornton: We can map that via Slack user groups + bot scopes. For example, the bot posts a summary, but “View diff” is only enabled for an allowed group.

[17:48] Elaine Chen: And if someone outside that group tries?

[17:51] Miles Thornton: They get a message like “Insufficient permissions; request access,” and it’s logged.

[17:58] Elaine Chen: Perfect.

[18:01] Dev Patel: Switching gears: were there any false leads because of missing saturation signals?

[18:07] Miguel Alvarez: Yeah, because GPU util was moderate, we assumed it wasn’t capacity, so we looked at network. But later it was basically queueing.

[18:17] Logan Wright: That’s why we want queue depth and scheduler saturation in the card. GPU util alone is misleading.

[18:26] Jordan Price: Also, our team debated whether to shift traffic to eu-west because we saw errors in us-west. If we had known eu-west was already tight, we wouldn’t.

[18:38] Logan Wright: Regional capacity signal can prevent that.

[18:41] Sarah Kim: Yep.

[18:43] Dev Patel: Okay. I think we have enough on requirements. Let’s outline the beta.

[18:50] Owen Phillips: Proposal: once we have the cards stable, we enable for Acme-prod incidents only, in two regions, for one on-call rotation. You give us feedback.

[19:03] Miguel Alvarez: Works.

[19:05] Sarah Kim: How soon?

[19:07] Miles Thornton: We’re aiming end of March for broader beta, but we can share mockups this week and confirm what you want before we flip anything.

[19:18] Jacob Stein: I can send screenshots of card layouts and sample redaction warnings.

[19:25] Elaine Chen: Please do. Especially the redaction behavior.

[19:29] Dev Patel: Logan, can you validate which saturation metrics we can expose safely to Acme? Specifically KV cache pressure and scheduler backpressure.

[19:38] Logan Wright: Yep. For dedicated pools, we should have them. I’ll confirm label set and ensure it’s tenant-safe.

[19:48] Anika Rao: What does “tenant-safe” mean here exactly?

[19:52] Logan Wright: Basically: the metric query only returns your tenant’s data, and the labels don’t include things that identify other tenants. Also we avoid raw per-request IDs.

[20:06] Anika Rao: Great.

[20:08] Jordan Price: Can the bot also show “queue depth at router” versus “queue depth at scheduler”? Because those mean different things.

[20:16] Logan Wright: We likely show one combined “effective queue depth” plus a drill-down link. Too much detail in the card gets confusing.

[20:26] Miguel Alvarez: Agreed. Keep the card short.

[20:29] Dev Patel: Elaine, you mentioned audit retention. Can you send your requirements? One year, anything else?

[20:36] Elaine Chen: One year, searchable by user and by incident ID. And we’ll need to know if you can export to us or at least provide reports.

[20:48] Owen Phillips: We can provide audit reports as part of the enterprise support package. Let’s follow up offline.

[20:55] Dev Patel: Cool.

[20:56] (brief pause)

[21:00] Sarah Kim: One more request—during incidents, we often ask “what changed in the last hour.” Can the bot automatically include a one-hour window for deploys and config?

[21:10] Jacob Stein: Yes. The default is “last 60 minutes” and we can let you expand to 6 hours.

[21:17] Miguel Alvarez: That’s good. Don’t go beyond that by default.

[21:22] Miles Thornton: And we’ll include timestamps in UTC and local? Or just UTC?

[21:28] Miguel Alvarez: UTC is fine, but be consistent.

[21:31] Dev Patel: Noted.

[21:34] Jordan Price: Also, can the bot include “known ongoing incident” banner? Like if Redwood already has a regional issue.

[21:43] Owen Phillips: We can include a link to our status page and internal incident ID if there is one. But we’re not overhauling status tooling as part of this.

[21:54] Sarah Kim: Even a link is fine.

[21:57] Dev Patel: Okay.

[22:00] Miguel Alvarez: I want to make sure you heard the key pain: we couldn’t decide between mitigation options because we didn’t know whether it was deploy regression, our config, or saturation.

[22:12] Dev Patel: Heard loud and clear. That’s literally the three cards.

[22:17] Jacob Stein: Quick check—would you want the bot to recommend “rollback deploy” vs “rollback config”? Or just present evidence?

[22:25] Sarah Kim: Evidence. Recommendations are risky.

[22:28] Miguel Alvarez: Yeah, just evidence.

[22:31] Logan Wright: We can highlight anomalies without prescribing.

[22:35] Dev Patel: Good.

[22:37] (crosstalk)

[22:40] Dev Patel: Anything else we missed?

[22:44] Anika Rao: Minor: include “streaming disconnects” metric if applicable. We saw some client-side drops.

[22:52] Logan Wright: That’s part of the SLO signal set. We can link to the streaming health dashboard.

[22:58] Jordan Price: Great.

[23:00] Dev Patel: Okay, we’ll do a quick recap and then give you time back.

[23:06] Dev Patel: Next steps: I’ll send the incident timeline export tomorrow. Jacob sends card mockups by Friday. Logan confirms saturation metrics availability and thresholds next week. Miles updates link templates.

[23:21] Dev Patel: Elaine, you send audit/access requirements and we’ll align on the permission model.

[23:28] Elaine Chen: Yes.

[23:30] Sarah Kim: Sounds good. Thanks all.

[23:33] Miguel Alvarez: Thanks. Looking forward to not doing Slack archeology next time.

[23:39] Owen Phillips: Same. Appreciate the candor.

[23:42] (Meeting continues with light scheduling and off-topic coordination; recording ends.)

source.content:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting Transcript

Title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
Date: 2025-02-25
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 43 min

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Daniel Cho, Maya Chen, Alex Nguyen

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed results from last week’s mitigations (concurrency cap + batch sizing guardrails) and early canary signals for TP stability fixes.
- Discussed runtime 1.19 rollout sequencing: Dedicated canary → expanded canary → production.
- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current state: timeout rate and p99 since mitigations
2) Canary readout: what improved / what still looks weird
3) Rollout plan and feature flags (watchdog, scheduler guardrail)
4) Success criteria + what data Redwood needs from Acme
5) Next steps / go-no go

Questions (auto-generated)
- Will the watchdog abort cause customer-visible errors? Is there retry behavior?
- What is the rollback trigger if p99 regresses but timeouts go down?
- Can Acme run with extra NCCL debug logging safely?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um thanks everyone for jumping on. This is our follow-up from last week’s deep dive on the TP hangs. Goal today is basically: (1) are the mitigations helping, (2) what did we see in the canary, and (3) agree on rollout timeline and success criteria.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:20] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all.

[00:22] Noah Patel: Hi.

[00:23] Rafael Mendes: Hey, Rafael from SRE.

[00:26] Daniel Cho: Daniel here.

[00:27] Alex Nguyen: Alex, ML.

[00:29] Maya Chen: Maya.

[00:31] Rachel Kim: Great. Priya, do you want to start with like, what you’ve seen since you applied the concurrency cap and the smaller batch? Like qualitatively and numbers if you have them.

[00:41] Priya Shah: Yeah. So we turned on the caps Friday night, basically we put the per-replica concurrency down… I think from 24 to 14? Daniel can correct me. And we also set the max prefill tokens per batch, and that reduced the like, “everything is stuck” events.

[00:59] Daniel Cho: Yeah, it was 24 to 12 actually on the busiest deployment. We did 14 on the less busy one. And the timeouts, I pulled the graph this morning. The request timeout rate dropped from around 0.8% at peak to like 0.15-ish. It’s not zero, but it’s like 5x better.

[01:17] Rachel Kim: That’s good.

[01:18] Daniel Cho: But p99 got a little worse, which makes sense because we’re throttling. Like p99 went from ~3.8 seconds to ~4.6 seconds on the chat endpoint.

[01:28] Maya Chen: Which is okay-ish for us for now, but not like… we can’t keep it there forever.

[01:34] Rachel Kim: Yeah totally. The point of the mitigation is “stop the bleeding” not the steady state.

[01:39] Noah Patel: And the pattern you’re describing matches what we’ve seen. The hang signature is triggered under a certain mix, so reducing concurrency reduces probability, but it also increases queuing.

[01:50] Priya Shah: Right.

[01:51] Rachel Kim: So that’s the baseline. Now, on our side, we shipped a canary build to a small set of Dedicated clusters last week with the scheduling guardrail and additional NCCL stream sync. We haven’t rolled it broad on Hosted yet. For Acme, we can do a canary on one replica group, TP=4, A100 80 gig, same as production.

[02:12] Alex Nguyen: That’s the same hardware we have, yeah.

[02:15] Rachel Kim: Noah, do you want to summarize what changed in the canary, in plain English.

[02:20] Noah Patel: Sure. So, three buckets. One: scheduler changes so collectives can’t be starved by prefill-heavy batches. Before, under certain interleavings, rank 0 could get ahead on compute and then wait on a collective that other ranks never reach because they’re stuck doing other work, and it looks like a “silent stall.” Two: stricter stream/event ordering around NCCL calls; some paths relied on implicit sync, which is not always safe especially with multi-stream decode. Three: instrumentation—heartbeat per rank, in-flight collective counters, and stall reason tags. And then the optional thing, which we haven’t defaulted on, is the watchdog timeout that aborts the step and drains the replica instead of waiting forever.

[03:04] Daniel Cho: When you say drains the replica, does that mean it kills the pod?

[03:08] Rafael Mendes: Not necessarily kill, but it removes it from service and we replace it. Practically, yes, you’ll see a drain event and then a restart depending on the environment policy.

[03:17] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[03:18] Maya Chen: And does that mean end-user sees an error?

[03:21] Rachel Kim: Potentially, yes, but a fast error rather than a 60-second hang then timeout. And we can route or retry at your client layer depending how you’re set up.

[03:30] Noah Patel: The idea is “fail closed” with a structured error code. It should be actionable: “tp_stall_watchdog_abort” basically, rather than generic 504.

[03:40] Priya Shah: That’s better than the long wait. We can handle retries if it’s not too frequent.
