[13:49] Noah Patel: Risk is reduced significantly. The main issue was indefinite stalls in TP that led to timeouts and autoscaling thrash. With ordering fixes and instrumentation, we can prevent and detect it. Watchdog is the backstop.

[14:05] Priya Shah: Ok.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: There’s also the question of rolling this to Hosted eventually, but for you this is Dedicated. We’ll do Dedicated-first.

[14:13] Maya Chen: Good.

[14:15] Rachel Kim: Alright, I’m going to skip ahead a bit—Noah, anything else you want them to know about the canary behavior.

[14:21] Noah Patel: Only that if you see a small throughput regression, like 1-2%, it’s expected due to stream sync. We can try to conditionalize later, but for now stability > micro perf.

[14:33] Alex Nguyen: Ok.

[14:34] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[14:36] Rachel Kim: Great. Let’s do next steps and then we can give you time back.

[14:41] Rachel Kim: Next steps: I send recap today with success criteria and schedule. Tomorrow: canary enabled 10% with the 1.19 build, watchdog off by default. Thursday: review results. Friday: expand if good. Monday: full rollout pending go/no-go.

[14:59] Priya Shah: Sounds right.

[15:01] Daniel Cho: Works.

[15:03] Rafael Mendes: I’ll make sure you have dashboard access and I’ll be on point during your peak window tomorrow.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Thank you.

[15:10] Liam O'Rourke: I’ll coordinate the actual traffic split change with our release folks.

[15:14] Rachel Kim: Awesome. Any last questions.

[15:17] Daniel Cho: Not from me.

[15:18] Priya Shah: All good.

[15:20] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[15:21] Rachel Kim: Great, thanks everyone.

[15:24] (Call continues with a few minutes of scheduling and side chatter; transcript ends.)

Notes
- Acme confirmed current mitigations (lower concurrency + batch guardrails) reduced timeouts ~5x but increased p99 latency.
- Redwood proposed canary tomorrow; focus on stability fixes without enabling watchdog initially.
- Agreed success criteria: timeout rate <0.05% at peak on canary slice, no sustained TP heartbeat gaps, and path to bring p99 back <4s by raising concurrency after stability is proven.
text:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting Transcript

Title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
Date: 2025-02-25
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 43 min

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Daniel Cho, Maya Chen, Alex Nguyen

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed results from last week’s mitigations (concurrency cap + batch sizing guardrails) and early canary signals for TP stability fixes.
- Discussed runtime 1.19 rollout sequencing: Dedicated canary → expanded canary → production.
- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current state: timeout rate and p99 since mitigations
2) Canary readout: what improved / what still looks weird
3) Rollout plan and feature flags (watchdog, scheduler guardrail)
4) Success criteria + what data Redwood needs from Acme
5) Next steps / go-no go

Questions (auto-generated)
- Will the watchdog abort cause customer-visible errors? Is there retry behavior?
- What is the rollback trigger if p99 regresses but timeouts go down?
- Can Acme run with extra NCCL debug logging safely?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um thanks everyone for jumping on. This is our follow-up from last week’s deep dive on the TP hangs. Goal today is basically: (1) are the mitigations helping, (2) what did we see in the canary, and (3) agree on rollout timeline and success criteria.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:20] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all.

[00:22] Noah Patel: Hi.

[00:23] Rafael Mendes: Hey, Rafael from SRE.

[00:26] Daniel Cho: Daniel here.

[00:27] Alex Nguyen: Alex, ML.

[00:29] Maya Chen: Maya.

[00:31] Rachel Kim: Great. Priya, do you want to start with like, what you’ve seen since you applied the concurrency cap and the smaller batch? Like qualitatively and numbers if you have them.

[00:41] Priya Shah: Yeah. So we turned on the caps Friday night, basically we put the per-replica concurrency down… I think from 24 to 14? Daniel can correct me. And we also set the max prefill tokens per batch, and that reduced the like, “everything is stuck” events.

[00:59] Daniel Cho: Yeah, it was 24 to 12 actually on the busiest deployment. We did 14 on the less busy one. And the timeouts, I pulled the graph this morning. The request timeout rate dropped from around 0.8% at peak to like 0.15-ish. It’s not zero, but it’s like 5x better.

[01:17] Rachel Kim: That’s good.

[01:18] Daniel Cho: But p99 got a little worse, which makes sense because we’re throttling. Like p99 went from ~3.8 seconds to ~4.6 seconds on the chat endpoint.

[01:28] Maya Chen: Which is okay-ish for us for now, but not like… we can’t keep it there forever.

[01:34] Rachel Kim: Yeah totally. The point of the mitigation is “stop the bleeding” not the steady state.

[01:39] Noah Patel: And the pattern you’re describing matches what we’ve seen. The hang signature is triggered under a certain mix, so reducing concurrency reduces probability, but it also increases queuing.

[01:50] Priya Shah: Right.

[01:51] Rachel Kim: So that’s the baseline. Now, on our side, we shipped a canary build to a small set of Dedicated clusters last week with the scheduling guardrail and additional NCCL stream sync. We haven’t rolled it broad on Hosted yet. For Acme, we can do a canary on one replica group, TP=4, A100 80 gig, same as production.

[02:12] Alex Nguyen: That’s the same hardware we have, yeah.

[02:15] Rachel Kim: Noah, do you want to summarize what changed in the canary, in plain English.

[02:20] Noah Patel: Sure. So, three buckets. One: scheduler changes so collectives can’t be starved by prefill-heavy batches. Before, under certain interleavings, rank 0 could get ahead on compute and then wait on a collective that other ranks never reach because they’re stuck doing other work, and it looks like a “silent stall.” Two: stricter stream/event ordering around NCCL calls; some paths relied on implicit sync, which is not always safe especially with multi-stream decode. Three: instrumentation—heartbeat per rank, in-flight collective counters, and stall reason tags. And then the optional thing, which we haven’t defaulted on, is the watchdog timeout that aborts the step and drains the replica instead of waiting forever.

[03:04] Daniel Cho: When you say drains the replica, does that mean it kills the pod?

[03:08] Rafael Mendes: Not necessarily kill, but it removes it from service and we replace it. Practically, yes, you’ll see a drain event and then a restart depending on the environment policy.

[03:17] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[03:18] Maya Chen: And does that mean end-user sees an error?

[03:21] Rachel Kim: Potentially, yes, but a fast error rather than a 60-second hang then timeout. And we can route or retry at your client layer depending how you’re set up.

[03:30] Noah Patel: The idea is “fail closed” with a structured error code. It should be actionable: “tp_stall_watchdog_abort” basically, rather than generic 504.

[03:40] Priya Shah: That’s better than the long wait. We can handle retries if it’s not too frequent.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly. So, canary results so far. Rafael, do you want to share what SRE saw on the Dedicated canary clusters?

[03:53] Rafael Mendes: Yep. On the two clusters we canaried—different customers, similar workloads—we saw timeouts drop materially. On one, request timeout rate during peak went from ~0.6% to ~0.05%. The big thing is the heartbeat gaps basically disappeared. We still saw occasional NCCL errors, but those are now surfaced and correlate with a drain event. So instead of the cluster getting into a wedged state for like 20 minutes, it recovers in, say, under 2 minutes.

[04:23] Daniel Cho: That’s good.

[04:24] Rafael Mendes: Perf impact: there was a small regression, like 1 to 2% throughput down, because of extra sync. But it’s within our error bars and, honestly, worth it for stability.

[04:36] Alex Nguyen: And did you run long context? We have like 16k prompts sometimes.

[04:41] Noah Patel: We did. The canary included long-context stress scenario. The hang signature was more likely with mixed long prefill and then streaming decode on other requests. With the scheduler guardrail, we didn’t reproduce the “decode never returns” issue.

[04:56] Priya Shah: So you’re saying it’s fixed.

[04:58] Noah Patel: I’m saying the top two signatures we’re targeting appear mitigated. I won’t say “all hangs are fixed” because TP is… you know. But the known failure modes are improved and we have detection now.

[05:10] Rachel Kim: Priya, Maya, what you care about is: will your customer-facing timeouts stop. Our confidence is higher now.

[05:18] Maya Chen: Ok. What’s the rollout timeline if we agree to canary.

[05:23] Rachel Kim: Proposal: we enable canary for you tomorrow on one replica group—like 10% of traffic or one deployment slice—run it for 24 hours including a peak. If results look good Thursday, we expand to 50% Friday, then full production early next week. That’s assuming no freeze window on your side.

[05:44] Priya Shah: We do have a release freeze starting next Thursday for our own product. So ideally we’d like it in before that or we wait.

[05:52] Rachel Kim: Got it. So we aim to be in prod by Monday/Tuesday.

[05:56] Daniel Cho: How risky is this? Like what’s the rollback plan.

[06:00] Rafael Mendes: Rollback is straightforward: we can revert the runtime version for that deployment slice and/or disable the feature flags. The biggest question is if the watchdog causes false positives and drains too aggressively.

[06:12] Daniel Cho: Right, that’s what I’m worried about. If it drains constantly, we’ll get flapping.

[06:17] Noah Patel: That’s why we don’t want it default-on at first. We can start with instrumentation + scheduler + sync, and leave watchdog off. Then if you still have a small residual hang rate, we can enable watchdog with conservative thresholds.

[06:32] Maya Chen: So without watchdog, you think timeouts go away?

[06:35] Noah Patel: For the hang signatures we saw, yes. Watchdog is more like a safety net for unknowns.

[06:41] Priya Shah: Ok. That seems reasonable.

[06:44] Rachel Kim: Daniel, when you say timeouts still exist at 0.15%… do you know if they are all the same pattern? Like do you see GPU stall, or could be client cancellations.

[06:55] Daniel Cho: That’s actually unclear. Some are client disconnect, because streaming. But some are server side 504s. We didn’t have enough instrumentation to tell if it’s NCCL or scheduler.

[07:06] Noah Patel: With the new metrics, you’ll be able to tell. Like you’ll see “stall_reason=comms” vs “stall_reason=scheduler_starvation” vs “compute_long” etc.

[07:15] Alex Nguyen: Are those in the console or do we have to scrape Prometheus.

[07:19] Rachel Kim: For Dedicated, you’ll see them in the Redwood Console dashboards we share with you, and we can also export. Rafael can confirm the exact panels.

[07:27] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, we can add you to the dashboard view. There’s a panel for “TP heartbeat gap seconds” and another for “in-flight collectives” which is basically a counter per rank. If one rank’s counter stops moving, that’s the smoking gun.

[07:42] Daniel Cho: Perfect.

[07:43] Maya Chen: Can we talk success criteria. Like what do we agree means “go.”

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yes. I drafted something but let’s align. My suggestion: during peak hour window, request timeout rate below 0.05% on the canary slice, and no sustained heartbeat gaps over, say, 30 seconds. Also p99 not worse than today’s mitigated baseline, ideally improves when we relax concurrency caps.

[08:08] Priya Shah: That’s fine. We also care about “stuck streams,” like the client waiting and then nothing.

[08:14] Noah Patel: Those should show up as heartbeat gaps or as “decode step exceeded” if watchdog is on.

[08:20] Daniel Cho: For p99, I’d like to get back under 4 seconds. The cap pushes it above.

[08:25] Rachel Kim: Yep. And part of the plan is: after stability is improved, we can increase concurrency gradually. We don’t need to go back to 24 immediately.

[08:36] Alex Nguyen: Also we need to confirm no quality changes. This is runtime not model, but we had one case with another vendor where tokens got weird.

[08:44] Noah Patel: This shouldn’t change model outputs; it’s scheduling and synchronization. But if you see anything odd, let us know.

[08:53] Maya Chen: Ok.

[08:55] Rachel Kim: Quick question: are you still doing TP=4 for all models? Or do you have a TP=2 slice.

[09:01] Priya Shah: For production it’s TP=4, single replica group per region. We tested TP=2 but throughput wasn’t enough.

[09:10] Rachel Kim: Ok.

[09:11] Daniel Cho: Another question: you mentioned extra NCCL logging. Do we need that for the canary.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Not unless we see something new. We can enable it for a short window if there’s an incident, but it’s noisy and has some overhead. We’ve been trying to keep it minimal.

[09:30] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[09:31] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk about the canary setup. Liam, do you want to cover how we route traffic.

[09:36] Liam O'Rourke: Sure. We can do it a couple ways. Easiest is: new runtime version on one deployment slice, then route a fixed percentage of requests by a hash. We can also do “shadow” but that’s more complicated. For what we’re doing—stability—you want real traffic.

[09:54] Priya Shah: Real traffic, yes.

[09:56] Liam O'Rourke: So we’ll do 10% by hash to canary, and you’ll be able to compare metrics side-by-side.

[10:04] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[10:06] Rachel Kim: If you’re good with it, we can start tomorrow morning your time.

[10:10] Priya Shah: Works.

[10:11] Maya Chen: Can you also share in writing what exactly changed. Like release notes.

[10:16] Noah Patel: Yeah. It’s basically: scheduler guardrail to prevent collective starvation; NCCL stream/event sync hardening; new metrics; and improved error surfacing. Watchdog is behind a flag.

[10:30] Rachel Kim: I’ll send a recap email with those bullets and the success criteria.

[10:35] Priya Shah: Great.

[10:36] Daniel Cho: I want to ask about the client cancel thing. We had some customers cancel requests in the UI. Could that trigger the hang.

[10:45] Noah Patel: Yes, we found a cancellation path bug in TP where one rank could exit early and another rank still expects a collective. That’s one of the fixes in this line of work. I believe it’s in the 1.19 bundle.

[10:59] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[11:00] Alex Nguyen: So do we need to change our client behavior.

[11:03] Noah Patel: Ideally no. Cancellation should be safe. But if you’re aggressively cancelling every token, that’s… unusual. How often do you see cancels.

[11:12] Daniel Cho: It’s like user navigates away, or they hit stop generation.

[11:16] Noah Patel: That should be fine.

[11:19] Rachel Kim: On the timeouts: you mentioned 0.15% after caps. If we canary tomorrow and see it drop further without raising p99, that’s a win.

[11:31] Maya Chen: Do we plan to remove the caps immediately.

[11:34] Rachel Kim: Not immediately. We’ll keep them during canary for apples-to-apples. Then once canary is stable, we raise concurrency in steps. Like 12 → 14 → 16, watching the stall metrics.

[11:47] Priya Shah: That’s sensible.

[11:50] Rafael Mendes: And from on-call perspective, we’ll also have new alerts. So if there is a stall signal, we drain the replica proactively instead of waiting for customer timeouts.

[12:01] Daniel Cho: That’s the piece we wanted.

[12:04] Rachel Kim: Any concerns from your side about doing the canary tomorrow.

[12:09] Priya Shah: Only the freeze timeline. But if we’re in prod by Monday, that’s fine.

[12:13] Maya Chen: Yeah.

[12:15] Alex Nguyen: We’ll need access to the dashboard.

[12:17] Rafael Mendes: I’ll coordinate that with you after this call.

[12:20] Rachel Kim: Ok. I also want to set expectations: even with the fixes, you may see an occasional drain event. But it should be rare and not cascade.

[12:30] Daniel Cho: Define rare.

[12:32] Noah Patel: In the canary we saw like, single digits per day at worst, and those were mostly correlated with real NCCL errors rather than stalls. The stalls went down drastically.

[12:44] Priya Shah: Ok.

[12:45] Maya Chen: And if we see drain events, does that show as a brief blip.

[12:49] Liam O'Rourke: If you have enough replicas, it’s masked. If you have one replica group with limited redundancy, you might see a small latency spike. But better than a long wedge.

[13:02] Priya Shah: Understood.

[13:04] Rachel Kim: Let’s do a quick recap of what we need from Acme during the canary. Daniel, we’ll ask for: request timeout rate, p95/p99, and if possible count of client cancels.

[13:16] Daniel Cho: Yes.

[13:17] Rachel Kim: And then if any errors happen, please paste the structured error code and request id so we can correlate.

[13:24] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[13:26] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can note when your peak traffic is—like hour window—so we can eyeball the graphs at the same time.

[13:34] Daniel Cho: Peak is 9am to 11am Pacific.

[13:38] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[13:39] Rachel Kim: Cool.

[13:41] Maya Chen: One more question: do you think this solves the enterprise customer impact we had last week. Like are we still at risk of a sev1.

[13:49] Noah Patel: Risk is reduced significantly. The main issue was indefinite stalls in TP that led to timeouts and autoscaling thrash. With ordering fixes and instrumentation, we can prevent and detect it. Watchdog is the backstop.

[14:05] Priya Shah: Ok.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: There’s also the question of rolling this to Hosted eventually, but for you this is Dedicated. We’ll do Dedicated-first.

[14:13] Maya Chen: Good.

[14:15] Rachel Kim: Alright, I’m going to skip ahead a bit—Noah, anything else you want them to know about the canary behavior.

[14:21] Noah Patel: Only that if you see a small throughput regression, like 1-2%, it’s expected due to stream sync. We can try to conditionalize later, but for now stability > micro perf.

[14:33] Alex Nguyen: Ok.

[14:34] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[14:36] Rachel Kim: Great. Let’s do next steps and then we can give you time back.

[14:41] Rachel Kim: Next steps: I send recap today with success criteria and schedule. Tomorrow: canary enabled 10% with the 1.19 build, watchdog off by default. Thursday: review results. Friday: expand if good. Monday: full rollout pending go/no-go.

[14:59] Priya Shah: Sounds right.

[15:01] Daniel Cho: Works.

[15:03] Rafael Mendes: I’ll make sure you have dashboard access and I’ll be on point during your peak window tomorrow.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Thank you.

[15:10] Liam O'Rourke: I’ll coordinate the actual traffic split change with our release folks.

[15:14] Rachel Kim: Awesome. Any last questions.

[15:17] Daniel Cho: Not from me.

[15:18] Priya Shah: All good.

[15:20] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[15:21] Rachel Kim: Great, thanks everyone.

[15:24] (Call continues with a few minutes of scheduling and side chatter; transcript ends.)

Notes
- Acme confirmed current mitigations (lower concurrency + batch guardrails) reduced timeouts ~5x but increased p99 latency.
- Redwood proposed canary tomorrow; focus on stability fixes without enabling watchdog initially.
- Agreed success criteria: timeout rate <0.05% at peak on canary slice, no sustained TP heartbeat gaps, and path to bring p99 back <4s by raising concurrency after stability is proven.

source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Date: 2025-02-18
Time: 18:30–19:28 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes
Meeting title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated TP hang technical deep dive
Call type: technical_deep_dive

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Maya Chen, Ethan Brooks, Priya Nair, Jonas Keller

Summary (auto-generated)
- Acme described intermittent request hangs/timeouts on Dedicated when running tensor-parallel replicas under peak load, mostly during streaming chat workloads with mixed prompt lengths.
- Redwood walked through likely failure modes (collective ordering mismatch/starvation, stream/event sync gaps around NCCL, cancellation edge cases) and what telemetry is needed to separate compute vs comms vs scheduler stalls.
- Agreed on immediate mitigations (reduce per-replica concurrency, cap prefill, temporarily disable specific fast-paths if enabled) and a plan to capture logs in a short debug window.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Symptoms and frequency of timeouts/hangs
- Workload characteristics (prefill/decode mix, long context, bursty concurrency)
- What to capture: request IDs, replica/rank context, NCCL debug, GPU metrics
- Mitigations and canary plan for upcoming runtime fixes

Questions (auto-generated)
- Is the hang correlated with streaming client disconnects?
- Are CUDA graphs enabled and if so, is capture interacting with NCCL stream ordering?
- Does the issue reproduce on smaller TP degree or only at TP=8?

Action items (auto-generated)
- Acme to share incident windows and logs
- Redwood to share debug capture instructions + mitigation config
- Follow-up meeting after artifact review

---

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, thanks everyone. This is Rachel from Redwood CS. We pulled in Noah and Hiro from runtime and Rafael from SRE. Goal today is to understand the TP hang behavior you’re seeing on Dedicated and leave with like, a concrete list of logs and immediate mitigations.

[00:18] Maya Chen: Yep, sounds good. Thanks for jumping on. This has been pretty disruptive for us.

[00:23] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all. Liam here, I’m the technical account liaison. I’ve been tracking the support tickets. Priya, I saw your note that the timeouts line up with specific replicas.

[00:33] Priya Nair: Yeah. We can show it. Basically, some replicas just… stop responding. The service is still “up” but requests hang until client timeout.

[00:42] Noah Patel: When you say hang, is it a single request stuck, or does the whole replica stop making forward progress, like queue grows and nothing completes?

[00:50] Priya Nair: The whole replica. Queue grows. We see upstream 504s from our gateway after 120 seconds. And then we drain the replica and it recovers.

[01:01] Hiro Tanaka: Got it. Is GPU utilization flat during the hang, or pegged?

[01:05] Ethan Brooks: Flat. We see a drop. It’s not like it’s doing compute slowly. It’s like stalled.

[01:12] Noah Patel: That’s consistent with a collective wait or a stream sync deadlock. Let’s get environment specifics. What’s the hardware and TP degree?

[01:20] Ethan Brooks: A100 80GB. Eight GPUs per node. Each replica uses eight, so TP=8. We have… four replicas in the pool.

[01:32] Maya Chen: Dedicated in your us-east region. It’s isolated, no shared capacity.

[01:36] Rafael Mendes: And you’re seeing it under sustained load, or only during spikes?

[01:41] Priya Nair: Mostly spikes. Like when we get bursty traffic, it starts. But we also saw it last night under steady load with a long-context job.

[01:52] Rachel Kim: Can you share a rough frequency? Like once a day, once a week?

[01:57] Priya Nair: Last week it was twice. Since Sunday, it’s been like… daily. We had a bigger launch.

[02:05] Liam O'Rourke: And you’re mostly streaming chat, right?

[02:08] Maya Chen: Yeah. It’s chat completions, streaming. We also do some summarization calls that are non-streaming.

[02:16] Noah Patel: Do you have a sense of mix? Like prefill heavy vs decode heavy?

[02:20] Ethan Brooks: Hmm. Most of our chat prompts are medium, 1k to 4k tokens input. But we have a chunk at 12k to 16k input because we do retrieval with a lot of text.

[02:35] Maya Chen: And output is like 200 to 800 tokens, usually.

[02:40] Noah Patel: Okay. Mixed prompt lengths plus streaming decode is exactly where scheduler edge cases show up.

[02:46] Hiro Tanaka: Are you using any cancellation? Like client disconnects, you stop generation?

[02:51] Priya Nair: Yes. Users close the tab, our gateway closes the stream. We send cancel. That’s common.

[02:58] Noah Patel: Important. We’ve seen a class of bugs where cancel leaves one rank not entering the next collective, and others wait.

[03:06] Maya Chen: That sounds like what we’re seeing. Is that fixed?

[03:10] Noah Patel: We have a fix in progress, but I want to confirm with evidence. We’ll need a trace ID around a hang where there was a cancellation.

[03:18] Rafael Mendes: From an ops perspective, it’d be helpful to know: when it happens, do you see any NCCL errors? Or is it silent?

[03:25] Priya Nair: Silent. No crash. No error. That’s the frustrating part.

[03:30] Hiro Tanaka: Silent stalls are usually stream/event ordering or collective mismatch without triggering timeout.

[03:36] Jonas Keller: Quick question on logging—if you ask us to turn on debug, we need to know exactly what’s logged and retention. We can’t dump customer prompts.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Totally. We’ll keep it to runtime-level logs, no prompt text. We can also do a short capture window and you can sanitize.

[03:54] Noah Patel: For our purposes, we need: request IDs, replica ID, and internal step markers, not content.

[04:02] Liam O'Rourke: Priya, can you describe the symptom timeline? Like do you see p99 creeping up first, then hang, or it’s sudden?

[04:10] Priya Nair: It’s pretty sudden. We get a burst, latency climbs. Then one replica goes “bad” and everything routed there times out. Our autoscaler sometimes makes it worse because it adds pressure elsewhere.

[04:23] Rafael Mendes: That matches what we’ve seen in other incidents—autoscaling thrash when one replica wedges.

[04:29] Noah Patel: What are your current per-replica concurrency settings? Like max in-flight requests.

[04:35] Ethan Brooks: I think we’re at 32 per replica. That was recommended earlier for throughput.

[04:41] Hiro Tanaka: For TP=8 and long contexts, 32 can be pretty aggressive, especially mixed prefill.

[04:47] Noah Patel: As a mitigation, we may want to drop to 16 temporarily. It’s not ideal for cost, but stability.

[04:54] Maya Chen: We can do that. But we need an estimate on impact.

[04:58] Noah Patel: Likely throughput drops, but p99 should improve and you reduce stall probability. We can also cap prefill tokens per batch.

[05:08] Ethan Brooks: Are those knobs on our side or on your side?

[05:11] Liam O'Rourke: For Dedicated, we can set them at the service config layer. We’ll send recommended values, you approve.

[05:18] Rachel Kim: We’ll frame it as an interim workaround, until runtime fixes land.

[05:24] Noah Patel: Let’s talk about model and runtime version. Which model and what runtime build are you on?

[15:14] Rafael Mendes: Immediate: drain the wedged replica, reduce routing to it, and temporarily lower concurrency. If you can’t drain fast, you can cap new inflight requests to keep blast radius smaller.

[15:26] Priya Nair: We can automate that.

[15:28] Noah Patel: And please grab the timestamp and a trace ID before draining, if possible.

[15:34] Priya Nair: Got it.

[15:36] Maya Chen: What’s the “best” mitigation if we have to pick one?

[15:39] Noah Patel: Concurrency reduction is the safest and quickest. It reduces the scheduler corner cases.

[15:46] Hiro Tanaka: Second is capping prefill batch packing.

[15:50] Maya Chen: Okay.

[15:52] Rachel Kim: We’ll send concrete numbers. We won’t leave it vague.

[15:56] Liam O'Rourke: And we’ll schedule a follow-up. How does Thursday work?

[16:00] Maya Chen: Thursday is fine.

[16:02] Priya Nair: Yep.

[16:04] Rachel Kim: Great. We’ll send notes and the checklist. Thanks everyone.

[16:10] (Crosstalk / end of main discussion; remaining recording contains light scheduling chatter and is omitted for brevity.)
text:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Date: 2025-02-18
Time: 18:30–19:28 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes
Meeting title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated TP hang technical deep dive
Call type: technical_deep_dive

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Maya Chen, Ethan Brooks, Priya Nair, Jonas Keller

Summary (auto-generated)
- Acme described intermittent request hangs/timeouts on Dedicated when running tensor-parallel replicas under peak load, mostly during streaming chat workloads with mixed prompt lengths.
- Redwood walked through likely failure modes (collective ordering mismatch/starvation, stream/event sync gaps around NCCL, cancellation edge cases) and what telemetry is needed to separate compute vs comms vs scheduler stalls.
- Agreed on immediate mitigations (reduce per-replica concurrency, cap prefill, temporarily disable specific fast-paths if enabled) and a plan to capture logs in a short debug window.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Symptoms and frequency of timeouts/hangs
- Workload characteristics (prefill/decode mix, long context, bursty concurrency)
- What to capture: request IDs, replica/rank context, NCCL debug, GPU metrics
- Mitigations and canary plan for upcoming runtime fixes

Questions (auto-generated)
- Is the hang correlated with streaming client disconnects?
- Are CUDA graphs enabled and if so, is capture interacting with NCCL stream ordering?
- Does the issue reproduce on smaller TP degree or only at TP=8?

Action items (auto-generated)
- Acme to share incident windows and logs
- Redwood to share debug capture instructions + mitigation config
- Follow-up meeting after artifact review

---

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, thanks everyone. This is Rachel from Redwood CS. We pulled in Noah and Hiro from runtime and Rafael from SRE. Goal today is to understand the TP hang behavior you’re seeing on Dedicated and leave with like, a concrete list of logs and immediate mitigations.

[00:18] Maya Chen: Yep, sounds good. Thanks for jumping on. This has been pretty disruptive for us.

[00:23] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all. Liam here, I’m the technical account liaison. I’ve been tracking the support tickets. Priya, I saw your note that the timeouts line up with specific replicas.

[00:33] Priya Nair: Yeah. We can show it. Basically, some replicas just… stop responding. The service is still “up” but requests hang until client timeout.

[00:42] Noah Patel: When you say hang, is it a single request stuck, or does the whole replica stop making forward progress, like queue grows and nothing completes?

[00:50] Priya Nair: The whole replica. Queue grows. We see upstream 504s from our gateway after 120 seconds. And then we drain the replica and it recovers.

[01:01] Hiro Tanaka: Got it. Is GPU utilization flat during the hang, or pegged?

[01:05] Ethan Brooks: Flat. We see a drop. It’s not like it’s doing compute slowly. It’s like stalled.

[01:12] Noah Patel: That’s consistent with a collective wait or a stream sync deadlock. Let’s get environment specifics. What’s the hardware and TP degree?

[01:20] Ethan Brooks: A100 80GB. Eight GPUs per node. Each replica uses eight, so TP=8. We have… four replicas in the pool.

[01:32] Maya Chen: Dedicated in your us-east region. It’s isolated, no shared capacity.

[01:36] Rafael Mendes: And you’re seeing it under sustained load, or only during spikes?

[01:41] Priya Nair: Mostly spikes. Like when we get bursty traffic, it starts. But we also saw it last night under steady load with a long-context job.

[01:52] Rachel Kim: Can you share a rough frequency? Like once a day, once a week?

[01:57] Priya Nair: Last week it was twice. Since Sunday, it’s been like… daily. We had a bigger launch.

[02:05] Liam O'Rourke: And you’re mostly streaming chat, right?

[02:08] Maya Chen: Yeah. It’s chat completions, streaming. We also do some summarization calls that are non-streaming.

[02:16] Noah Patel: Do you have a sense of mix? Like prefill heavy vs decode heavy?

[02:20] Ethan Brooks: Hmm. Most of our chat prompts are medium, 1k to 4k tokens input. But we have a chunk at 12k to 16k input because we do retrieval with a lot of text.

[02:35] Maya Chen: And output is like 200 to 800 tokens, usually.

[02:40] Noah Patel: Okay. Mixed prompt lengths plus streaming decode is exactly where scheduler edge cases show up.

[02:46] Hiro Tanaka: Are you using any cancellation? Like client disconnects, you stop generation?

[02:51] Priya Nair: Yes. Users close the tab, our gateway closes the stream. We send cancel. That’s common.

[02:58] Noah Patel: Important. We’ve seen a class of bugs where cancel leaves one rank not entering the next collective, and others wait.

[03:06] Maya Chen: That sounds like what we’re seeing. Is that fixed?

[03:10] Noah Patel: We have a fix in progress, but I want to confirm with evidence. We’ll need a trace ID around a hang where there was a cancellation.

[03:18] Rafael Mendes: From an ops perspective, it’d be helpful to know: when it happens, do you see any NCCL errors? Or is it silent?

[03:25] Priya Nair: Silent. No crash. No error. That’s the frustrating part.

[03:30] Hiro Tanaka: Silent stalls are usually stream/event ordering or collective mismatch without triggering timeout.

[03:36] Jonas Keller: Quick question on logging—if you ask us to turn on debug, we need to know exactly what’s logged and retention. We can’t dump customer prompts.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Totally. We’ll keep it to runtime-level logs, no prompt text. We can also do a short capture window and you can sanitize.

[03:54] Noah Patel: For our purposes, we need: request IDs, replica ID, and internal step markers, not content.

[04:02] Liam O'Rourke: Priya, can you describe the symptom timeline? Like do you see p99 creeping up first, then hang, or it’s sudden?

[04:10] Priya Nair: It’s pretty sudden. We get a burst, latency climbs. Then one replica goes “bad” and everything routed there times out. Our autoscaler sometimes makes it worse because it adds pressure elsewhere.

[04:23] Rafael Mendes: That matches what we’ve seen in other incidents—autoscaling thrash when one replica wedges.

[04:29] Noah Patel: What are your current per-replica concurrency settings? Like max in-flight requests.

[04:35] Ethan Brooks: I think we’re at 32 per replica. That was recommended earlier for throughput.

[04:41] Hiro Tanaka: For TP=8 and long contexts, 32 can be pretty aggressive, especially mixed prefill.

[04:47] Noah Patel: As a mitigation, we may want to drop to 16 temporarily. It’s not ideal for cost, but stability.

[04:54] Maya Chen: We can do that. But we need an estimate on impact.

[04:58] Noah Patel: Likely throughput drops, but p99 should improve and you reduce stall probability. We can also cap prefill tokens per batch.

[05:08] Ethan Brooks: Are those knobs on our side or on your side?

[05:11] Liam O'Rourke: For Dedicated, we can set them at the service config layer. We’ll send recommended values, you approve.

[05:18] Rachel Kim: We’ll frame it as an interim workaround, until runtime fixes land.

[05:24] Noah Patel: Let’s talk about model and runtime version. Which model and what runtime build are you on?

[05:30] Ethan Brooks: Model is “Llama 70B instruct” variant. I don’t remember the exact tag. Runtime version is 1.18.something.

[05:39] Priya Nair: 1.18.7 I think.

[05:41] Noah Patel: Okay. We’re working toward a 1.19 release that includes multiple TP stability hardening items.

[05:48] Hiro Tanaka: One change is stricter stream/event synchronization around NCCL collectives. It can add a tiny overhead but prevents silent stalls.

[05:56] Maya Chen: Is this related to CUDA graphs? Someone mentioned graphs in another thread.

[06:00] Hiro Tanaka: Potentially. Graph capture constrains stream semantics. If something tries to do collectives outside the captured ordering, it can stall.

[06:09] Ethan Brooks: I think graphs are enabled? Not 100%.

[06:13] Noah Patel: Can you confirm? It’s key. We’ve seen “fast path” modes that are safe in single GPU but tricky in TP.

[06:20] Ethan Brooks: I’ll check after this.

[06:22] Rachel Kim: We’ll put that as an action item.

[06:25] Rafael Mendes: On the logging side: for one of the incidents, can you share the exact time range and which replica IDs were drained?

[06:33] Priya Nair: Yes. The big one was Feb 17 around 03:10 UTC. Replica 2 wedged. We drained at like 03:22.

[06:44] Rafael Mendes: Great. If you can export logs from 03:10–03:35, that’s enough.

[06:50] Noah Patel: Also, any metrics snapshot: GPU util, GPU memory, and if you have request queue depth.

[06:56] Priya Nair: We have Prometheus. We can pull GPU metrics from DCGM and our gateway metrics.

[07:02] Noah Patel: Perfect. Redwood-side we also have scheduler queue depth but we need correlation.

[07:07] Maya Chen: What exactly do you want us to capture? Like a checklist.

[07:11] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll send a short checklist. But roughly: time window, request IDs that timed out, any client disconnect logs, and the replica identity.

[07:21] Noah Patel: And if you can, one request that was in-flight when it hung and never completed.

[07:26] Priya Nair: We can grab a trace ID from our logs.

[07:30] Hiro Tanaka: If we do a controlled debug window, we might ask to enable NCCL debug. But it’s noisy.

[07:37] Jonas Keller: That’s the part I want to understand. What does NCCL debug contain? Any payload?

[07:43] Hiro Tanaka: No payload. It’s mostly rank-to-rank operations, channel setup, collective names, and sometimes topology. Still can be sensitive in terms of infrastructure details.

[07:55] Rafael Mendes: We can do it for 10 minutes during off-peak, then turn it off and rotate logs.

[08:02] Jonas Keller: Okay. As long as it’s time-bounded and we know where it’s stored.

[08:07] Rachel Kim: We’ll document retention and who can access.

[08:11] Noah Patel: Let’s dig into the “mixed prefill/decode” part. Are you seeing hangs more when there are long-context requests entering while lots of streaming decodes are ongoing?

[08:22] Ethan Brooks: Yes. When a big RAG prompt comes in, it spikes. Then things get weird.

[08:28] Noah Patel: That points to scheduler starvation. If prefill batches monopolize scheduling, decode collectives might not get consistent ordering across ranks.

[08:39] Maya Chen: Can you explain that a bit? Like in English.

[08:42] Noah Patel: Sure. In TP, every GPU rank needs to do the same collective operations in the same order. If one rank ends up doing “prefill path A” while another is trying to do “decode path B” at that moment because of scheduling differences, they can disagree on the next collective and stall.

[08:58] Maya Chen: So it’s basically a synchronization bug.

[09:00] Noah Patel: Exactly. And it shows up only under certain queue mixes.

[09:06] Hiro Tanaka: It can also be triggered if one stream is waiting on an event that never gets recorded because of a cancellation path.

[09:13] Priya Nair: That matches “silent”. No crash.

[09:16] Rafael Mendes: Do you page on it today, or only after customers complain?

[09:20] Priya Nair: Right now it’s mostly customer complaints and our own error budget alarms. We page when 504 rate spikes.

[09:28] Rafael Mendes: Once we add heartbeat metrics on our side, we can alert earlier. But short term, mitigation is draining quickly when detected.

[09:37] Maya Chen: We already do that manually. We’d love an automatic signal.

[09:41] Noah Patel: Understood. We’re adding a per-rank heartbeat and stall reason codes. That should make it obvious “comms vs compute vs scheduler”.

[09:51] Liam O'Rourke: For your immediate operations, we can also wire “stall detected -> stop routing new requests to that replica” as a guardrail. That’s part of the plan.

[10:02] Maya Chen: That would help a lot.

[10:05] Rachel Kim: On mitigations, besides dropping concurrency, there’s also limiting long-context requests, like a separate pool. Is that something you can do?

[10:14] Maya Chen: Possibly. We can route long-context to a different tier, but it’s non-trivial.

[10:19] Noah Patel: Then let’s start with: reduce per-replica concurrency, cap prefill batch size, and if graphs are enabled we might temporarily disable for TP.

[10:30] Ethan Brooks: Disabling graphs might hurt throughput.

[10:33] Hiro Tanaka: It can, yes. We won’t recommend it unless we confirm it’s implicated.

[10:38] Noah Patel: We can do a staged approach: first concurrency + prefill caps. If hangs persist, we try disabling the graph capture on TP replicas.

[10:49] Priya Nair: What values do you recommend? Like 16 concurrency, what about prefill?

[10:54] Noah Patel: Tentatively: max in-flight 16; cap prefill tokens per batch, something like 8k per step (depends on your context). We’ll confirm with your exact distributions.

[11:07] Maya Chen: Our max input is 16k.

[11:10] Noah Patel: Then we can set a cap so the scheduler doesn’t try to pack too many long-prefills together.

[11:16] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll send this as “temporary config” and you can approve for your Dedicated deployment.

[11:22] Priya Nair: Okay.

[11:24] Rafael Mendes: For the incident windows: besides Feb 17, do you have another?

[11:29] Priya Nair: Feb 18 around 01:45 UTC. Similar pattern.

[11:34] Rafael Mendes: Great. Same ask: logs + IDs.

[11:38] Noah Patel: Do you have any client-side cancellations around that time? Like a spike in disconnect.

[11:43] Priya Nair: Yes. We had a deploy and some clients were reconnecting. So yeah, likely.

[11:50] Noah Patel: That’s useful.

[11:52] Jonas Keller: One more security note: if you’re asking for logs, we need to avoid anything that includes request content or customer identifiers.

[12:00] Rachel Kim: Agreed. We’ll keep it to metadata. And if anything includes raw prompts, you can redact before sharing.

[12:06] Liam O'Rourke: We can also accept just trace IDs and we’ll pull Redwood-side logs on our end, so you don’t have to export everything.

[12:14] Priya Nair: That’s preferable.

[12:17] Noah Patel: Yes, trace IDs plus timestamps are huge.

[12:21] Maya Chen: Can we talk timeline? When do we get the runtime fix?

[12:25] Noah Patel: We’re targeting runtime 1.19 canary in the next couple weeks. But we can also hotfix specific components if needed.

[12:35] Hiro Tanaka: For example, stream/event sync hardening is in review. There’s also a scheduler guardrail to prevent collective starvation.

[12:44] Maya Chen: Would that require downtime?

[12:46] Liam O'Rourke: For Dedicated, we can do rolling drain/replace of replicas. So minimal disruption, but there is risk.

[12:53] Rafael Mendes: We typically do canary on one replica first, observe 24–48 hours, then roll.

[13:00] Priya Nair: Okay.

[13:02] Noah Patel: Also, we’re adding a watchdog timeout for TP steps. Instead of hanging indefinitely, it will abort, drain the replica, and surface an actionable error.

[13:14] Maya Chen: That sounds scary. Could it false-positive and kill good work?

[13:18] Noah Patel: That’s the tradeoff. We’ll start with conservative thresholds and canary. But right now your clients time out anyway; this makes it bounded and recoverable.

[13:30] Priya Nair: Honestly bounded failure is better than silent.

[13:33] Rachel Kim: +1.

[13:35] Hiro Tanaka: And we can make it feature-flagged.

[13:38] Noah Patel: Exactly.

[13:40] Maya Chen: What do you need from us to validate the fix?

[13:44] Noah Patel: A reproducible workload profile. If you can share anonymized distributions: input length histogram, output lengths, concurrency pattern, and percent streaming.

[13:56] Ethan Brooks: We can share histograms. Not raw content.

[14:00] Noah Patel: Perfect.

[14:02] Liam O'Rourke: We can also run a stress scenario in our lab that matches those distributions.

[14:08] Priya Nair: Do you have a repro harness?

[14:10] Noah Patel: Yes, internally. We’re building a deterministic mixed prefill/decode repro. Your data helps tune it.

[14:18] Maya Chen: Great.

[14:20] Rafael Mendes: Quick check: do you see any node-level issues? Like PCIe errors, ECC, anything hardware.

[14:26] Priya Nair: No. We looked at nvidia-smi logs and it’s clean.

[14:30] Rafael Mendes: Good, that makes it more likely software ordering.

[14:35] Hiro Tanaka: One more: are you using multi-stream execution or any custom settings? Sometimes the overlap between compute stream and comm stream matters.

[14:43] Ethan Brooks: We haven’t changed anything from defaults, I think.

[14:46] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll confirm your current config snapshot.

[14:50] Rachel Kim: So, to recap what we’re asking for. Priya: two incident windows, trace IDs, and which replicas drained. Ethan: confirm graphs enabled and model tag. Maya: share histograms. Jonas: we’ll send a secure logging plan.

[15:06] Jonas Keller: Yep.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Before we end, can you tell us what to do if it happens again tonight? Like immediate steps.

[15:14] Rafael Mendes: Immediate: drain the wedged replica, reduce routing to it, and temporarily lower concurrency. If you can’t drain fast, you can cap new inflight requests to keep blast radius smaller.

[15:26] Priya Nair: We can automate that.

[15:28] Noah Patel: And please grab the timestamp and a trace ID before draining, if possible.

[15:34] Priya Nair: Got it.

[15:36] Maya Chen: What’s the “best” mitigation if we have to pick one?

[15:39] Noah Patel: Concurrency reduction is the safest and quickest. It reduces the scheduler corner cases.

[15:46] Hiro Tanaka: Second is capping prefill batch packing.

[15:50] Maya Chen: Okay.

[15:52] Rachel Kim: We’ll send concrete numbers. We won’t leave it vague.

[15:56] Liam O'Rourke: And we’ll schedule a follow-up. How does Thursday work?

[16:00] Maya Chen: Thursday is fine.

[16:02] Priya Nair: Yep.

[16:04] Rachel Kim: Great. We’ll send notes and the checklist. Thanks everyone.

[16:10] (Crosstalk / end of main discussion; remaining recording contains light scheduling chatter and is omitted for brevity.)

source.transcript:
Fireflies.ai transcription

Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Route Experiments (A/B) beta technical deep dive
Date: 2025-02-06
Time: 10:02 AM PT
Duration: 58 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Marissa Cole, Jada Williams, Logan Wright, Allison Grant
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Alex Kim, Dana Lopez, Samir Patel

Summary (auto-generated)
- Walked through route-level experiments beta: how deterministic assignment works, what to use for stickiness keys, and how experiment precedence interacts with rollouts/fallback.
- Reviewed metrics and attribution: latency percentiles, error rate, throughput, and cost/token definitions; discussed the “attribution drop rate” failure mode.
- Covered guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop behavior, default thresholds, and recommended ramp plan.
- Agreed on next steps to enable the beta flag and start a small ramp on Acme’s primary chat route.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Stickiness keys and deterministic bucketing
- Variant configuration and traffic splits
- Metrics definitions (latency, cost, errors) and attribution schema
- Guardrails, rollback triggers, and operating experiments safely
- Beta enablement and timeline

Transcript

[00:00] Marissa Cole: Alright, I think we’re recording. Hi everyone. Thanks for making time. This is the route experiments beta technical deep dive. Goal is to make sure you all are unblocked on stickiness, metrics, and guardrails before we flip the flag.

[00:14] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:16] Marissa Cole: Quick intros? I’m Marissa, product lead for Console and Rollouts.

[00:20] Jada Williams: Jada, I’m the backend architect on the rollouts and the A/B routing layer.

[00:24] Logan Wright: Logan, telemetry lead—metrics, tracing, attribution.

[00:27] Allison Grant: Allison, observability standards, work with SRE on dashboards and alerts.

[00:31] Priya Shah: Priya, head of ML at Acme. Dana is ML eng, Alex is platform, Samir SRE.

[00:37] Alex Kim: Hey.

[00:38] Dana Lopez: Hi.

[00:39] Samir Patel: Hello.

[00:41] Marissa Cole: Great. Priya, do you want to frame what you’re trying to do in the beta?

[00:46] Priya Shah: Yeah. We basically want to A/B model variants on a couple endpoints. Our biggest is chat completions. We want to compare, like, model A vs model B, but we don’t want user A to get different models every request. Also we care about cost. We’re currently doing some of this in-house but it’s… kind of brittle.

[01:06] Marissa Cole: Makes sense. And just to set expectations: in the beta, we’re giving you the route-level experiment with deterministic assignment and traffic splits and the core metrics in Console. We’re not doing full stats significance. You’ll get deltas and guardrail status.

[01:20] Priya Shah: Yep. That’s fine.

[01:22] Marissa Cole: Cool. Jada, can you walk through the assignment and stickiness model?

[01:27] Jada Williams: Sure. So, conceptually: an experiment attaches to a route, like /v1/chat/completions, and has variants—each variant points to a target policy, typically a model + config. Then at request time the policy engine evaluates: is there an active experiment for this route, is the request eligible, and if yes, compute a bucket.

[01:50] Jada Williams: The bucket is deterministic. We take a stickiness key—basically a string—we hash it with experiment_id (and a salt) and we land in [0, 1). Then we map that to your allocation, like 90/10 or 50/50.

[02:05] Alex Kim: When you say stickiness key, what do you expect us to send?

[02:09] Jada Williams: In beta we support a few options. The highest precedence is an explicit header you can send—internally we call it the experiment sticky key header. Sometimes folks mis-hear it as “stick key” or “sticky header.” It’s basically “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key”. If you send that, we use it.

[02:28] Dana Lopez: Is it per experiment or global?

[02:31] Jada Williams: It’s global per request. If you want per-experiment control, you can encode it yourself, like “user123:expABC”, but we usually recommend keep it stable per user or per session. The experiment_id is already in the hash input, so you don’t need to include it.

[02:50] Samir Patel: What if we don’t send it?

[02:52] Jada Williams: Then we try to fall back. If you’re using our SDKs, they can pass through a stable identifier if you provide it. Otherwise the router may fall back to something like an account id or request id. But—and this is important—if we fall back to a low entropy value, you can get skew.

[03:10] Priya Shah: Skew like one variant gets everything?

[03:12] Jada Williams: Exactly. If the fallback key is constant for a lot of traffic, the hash collapses. We’ve added validation and warnings in Console for “missing stickiness key” because we saw that in early testing.

[03:26] Alex Kim: We can send user_id reliably for logged-in traffic. For anonymous, we have session cookie.

[03:33] Jada Williams: That’s perfect. For anonymous, session is usually fine. Just make sure it’s stable across retries and across streaming. There’s a subtlety: streaming requests can have retry paths that don’t copy all headers if your gateway is doing something weird. So we want to verify your stickiness header makes it end-to-end.

[03:52] Logan Wright: And it matters for attribution too. If the assignment changes mid-stream—like first chunk from variant A and retry chunk from “no variant”—it can look like missing attribution.

[04:03] Dana Lopez: Okay. So, “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key”. Are there any other headers? Like can we force a variant for debugging?

[04:11] Jada Williams: In beta, we do have an internal-only override header for support, but we generally don’t expose it externally because it can break the integrity of experiments. What we do expose: response headers that tell you what you got assigned to. So you’ll see something like “X-Redwood-Experiment-Id” and “X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant”. I’m not 100% sure we spelled variant with “Variant-Id” or just “Variant”. Marissa, do you remember?

[04:35] Marissa Cole: In Console and docs it’s “X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant-Id”. But yeah, in some earlier builds it was shortened. We’ll confirm what your account will see when we enable.

[04:45] Alex Kim: That’d be super helpful for logging.

[04:48] Marissa Cole: Yep.

[04:49] Priya Shah: How does this interact with rollouts? Because we do canaries sometimes. Like canary a model version, and also run an experiment.

[04:59] Jada Williams: Good question. The precedence is: experiments evaluate at the route routing layer before fallback policies. So first we decide: is this request in experiment, and which variant. Then within that variant, we still apply standard safety checks—like model compatibility, region availability—and then fallback if necessary. 

[05:19] Samir Patel: So fallback can override the variant?

[05:22] Jada Williams: It can, but we record it. The ideal is fallback is rare. If fallback is happening a lot, your experiment results are hard to interpret.

[05:31] Allison Grant: And we show “fallback rate” and “variant effective traffic” in dashboards. One of the alerts is basically “variant got 0 traffic” or “variant traffic unexpectedly low.”

[05:42] Priya Shah: And canary rollouts—like model version canary—where does that sit?

[05:46] Jada Williams: In terms of policy graph: if your variant points at a rollout policy that itself has canary behavior, you can do it, but it’s easy to make it confusing. For beta we recommend keep variants simple: variant A = model X pinned, variant B = model Y pinned. If you want canary within a variant, we can talk, but I’d keep it out of the first experiment.

[06:10] Marissa Cole: Also, to be transparent, we’re still finalizing some UX copy around “overlapping experiments” and “experiments + rollouts,” but the core routing behavior is stable.

[06:20] Alex Kim: Are overlapping experiments allowed? Like two experiments on same route?

[06:25] Jada Williams: Not in the beta. We block it. One active experiment per route. We might add priority later, but not now.

[06:34] Priya Shah: Good.

[06:36] Marissa Cole: Let’s shift to metrics. Logan, can you outline what you emit and what we’ll see in Console?

[06:43] Logan Wright: Yeah. There are two parts: attribution and aggregation. Attribution means: every request/span has tags: route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region. Then we emit request-level metrics—latency and error—and token-level or generation-level metrics—tokens in/out, cost units.

[07:06] Logan Wright: In Console you’ll see the standard set per variant: p50/p95/p99 latency. Error rate—HTTP 5xx plus a bucket for “model error” like upstream timeouts. Throughput—requests per minute and tokens per second. And cost, which is the most confusing one.

[07:27] Dana Lopez: Yeah, what is “cost” exactly? Is it dollars, tokens, GPU seconds?

[07:32] Logan Wright: In Console today we show “estimated cost” in dollars based on your contracted rates, and “cost per 1K output tokens” as a normalized metric. Under the hood it’s derived from tokens in/out and your price schedule.

[07:48] Priya Shah: We care about cost per request too.

[07:50] Logan Wright: You’ll get that as “cost/request” and “tokens/request.” But keep in mind: if variants produce different output lengths, cost changes, and that’s real. The trick is: you also need to compare quality so you don’t just pick the shorter one.

[08:06] Priya Shah: Right. For quality, we have our own eval set.

[08:09] Marissa Cole: In beta we can attach eval signals if you run through our eval harness, but it’s optional. You can still use your own and just monitor latency/cost/errors.

[08:18] Dana Lopez: We probably will do your harness if it’s not too much work.

[08:22] Marissa Cole: Cool, we can do that later in phase 2 of your beta usage.

[08:27] Samir Patel: On latency, is it end-to-end or model only? Because we have some client-side time too.

[08:33] Logan Wright: What we show is server-side. Specifically: request received at edge to response complete. For streaming we track time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token separately. Sometimes the labels are “TTFT” and “TTLT”.

[08:50] Alex Kim: Good. TTFT is our big KPI.

[08:53] Logan Wright: Yep. For the percentiles we do p50/p95/p99 of TTFT and also overall latency. In the current Console view, you may need to toggle which chart you’re looking at.

[09:07] Allison Grant: And guardrails can be based on p95 or p99 depending on what you choose.

[09:12] Priya Shah: What’s the aggregation window? Like near real-time?

[09:15] Logan Wright: Near real-time is “few minutes” but not instantaneous. The rollup is in 1-minute buckets and then the dashboard shows 5-min or 15-min smoothing. There is sometimes a delay if the aggregation jobs are behind. We also show a “data freshness” indicator.

[09:34] Samir Patel: If the job is behind, does that affect guardrails auto stop?

[09:38] Allison Grant: Good question. Guardrails use the same underlying rollups but with a bit of a conservative window. For beta we treat auto-stop as optional.

[09:48] Marissa Cole: That’s actually a key point: in the beta, default behavior is “soft-stop”—we alert and mark the experiment as degraded, and we recommend you stop it in Console. Hard-stop is available but behind an additional switch.

[10:03] Priya Shah: We prefer soft-stop at first. False positives scare us.

[10:07] Allison Grant: Same. We’ve seen cases where a regional incident triggers p99 spikes for a few minutes and you don’t want everything to stop and flap.

[10:17] Dana Lopez: So what guardrails exist out of the box?

[10:20] Allison Grant: Defaults are: p99 latency regression threshold, error rate threshold, and cost per 1K tokens threshold. Also an “attribution drop rate” guardrail, which is more of a data quality thing.

[10:34] Alex Kim: What’s attribution drop rate?

[10:37] Logan Wright: It’s the percentage of requests on a route that should have experiment_id/variant_id but don’t. Like if the router assigned variant A but the spans downstream didn’t carry the tags, or streaming retry paths lost context.

[10:52] Jada Williams: Or if the client didn’t send the sticky key and the router fell back weirdly—some requests might not be considered eligible or might be in “control” without being tagged as control.

[11:06] Priya Shah: So basically “your measurement is broken.”

[11:08] Logan Wright: Exactly.

[11:10] Samir Patel: What thresholds do you use?

[11:13] Allison Grant: For beta, we generally start warning at 0.5% and alert at 2%, but it depends on volume. We can tune per account. Also we treat it as “do not trust results” rather than “service is down.”

[11:28] Samir Patel: Okay.

[11:30] Marissa Cole: On guardrail semantics: you can configure “stop experiment” vs “notify only.” Stop experiment in beta means stop routing traffic to variants and revert to your baseline policy.

[11:43] Alex Kim: Is it immediate?

[11:45] Jada Williams: It’s near-immediate, but there is config propagation. We have an SLO for config staleness; typical is under a minute, worst case a few minutes. There’s a propagation lag metric.

[12:00] Samir Patel: So you might “stop” but still see some variant traffic for a bit.

[12:03] Jada Williams: Exactly. And we log the config version used for assignment.

[12:10] Priya Shah: Does Console show that? Like the config version?

[12:13] Marissa Cole: In the admin/debug view, yes. For most users it’s hidden.

[12:18] Alex Kim: We’d want it for troubleshooting.

[12:21] Marissa Cole: We can grant the right RBAC role. There’s a permission for “view experiment debug details.” It’s not fully self-serve yet but we can set it up.

[12:33] Dana Lopez: What’s the recommended ramp plan?

[12:36] Allison Grant: We recommend: create experiment at 0% first (or 1% depending on UI), validate attribution and variant headers, then ramp 1% → 5% → 10% → 25% with at least a few hours between steps. For high-risk routes, 24 hours at 10%.

[12:58] Priya Shah: We can do 1% for a day. Our traffic is high.

[13:02] Logan Wright: High traffic makes the metrics more stable, which is nice.

[13:06] Alex Kim: Can we split by region? Like do experiment only in us-east first.

[13:11] Jada Williams: Yes, there are eligibility rules. In beta we support a simple region allowlist and maybe a header-based rule. If you want “only these tenants” we can do that too, but we should define the key.

[13:27] Priya Shah: Start with region only.

[13:30] Marissa Cole: That’s easiest.

[13:32] Dana Lopez: How do we define the variants? Is it only model id, or can we change parameters too?

[13:38] Jada Williams: Variant can point to a full routing target: model + quantization profile + batching config, basically whatever you can configure today for a route policy. But again, for interpretability, change one thing at a time.

[13:55] Priya Shah: We want to compare two model IDs first.

[13:58] Jada Williams: Great.

[14:00] Marissa Cole: Another question we’ve gotten is: what about users switching between variants when traffic split changes. Assignment is deterministic; traffic split just changes cut points. That means if you change from 90/10 to 50/50, some users will “move” variants.

[14:19] Alex Kim: That’s okay.

[14:20] Priya Shah: Yeah, expected.

[14:22] Samir Patel: Is there any “sticky” guarantee across days if the experiment restarts?

[14:27] Jada Williams: If you keep experiment_id the same, yes. If you stop and create a new experiment, no. So if you want continuity, pause rather than delete.

[14:40] Priya Shah: Makes sense.

[14:41] Logan Wright: Also: for cost metrics, make sure you understand if you’re looking at “per request” vs “per token.” Some teams accidentally optimize “cost/request” by forcing shorter responses but quality drops.

[14:55] Priya Shah: We’ve done that mistake before.

[14:57] Dana Lopez: Yeah.

[15:00] Marissa Cole: We can talk about quality signals briefly. Priya, do you want Redwood eval integration now or later?

[15:07] Priya Shah: Later. We have internal scoring and we’re not ready to push prompts outside that system yet.

[15:14] Marissa Cole: Totally fine. We can just start with latency/cost/errors and manual review.

[15:20] Alex Kim: On privacy: the stickiness key header—should it be hashed? We can’t send raw user id sometimes.

[15:28] Jada Williams: It can be hashed. We treat it as opaque. Just keep it stable. Use a keyed hash if you want.

[15:37] Samir Patel: Do you store it?

[15:39] Logan Wright: We do not store the raw sticky key in metrics. We use it at request time for bucketing. In logs, we also avoid recording it by default. The debug endpoint can show “hash input present” but it doesn’t echo the full key.

[15:54] Marissa Cole: And audit logs: experiment config changes are captured—who created, started, updated split, stopped, etc.

[16:03] Priya Shah: Good. That matters for us.

[16:06] Samir Patel: On audit logs, are they exportable? Like to our SIEM.

[16:11] Marissa Cole: In GA yes; in beta we have a basic export and we can provide it on request. We’ll follow up with the exact fields. For now, you’ll at least have the change history in Console.

[16:26] Alex Kim: Okay.

[16:28] Dana Lopez: One more on deterministic bucketing: what hash algorithm? Is it consistent across regions?

[16:34] Jada Williams: We use a stable hash—murmur-style, not crypto—plus a salt. It’s consistent across regions as long as the same config version is present. That’s why propagation lag matters. But the algorithm itself is consistent.

[16:51] Priya Shah: Great.

[16:52] Samir Patel: If we have multi-region active-active, can the same user hit different region and get different variant because of config lag?

[17:00] Jada Williams: In theory yes, during propagation windows. Practically, we keep it tight. If you want stricter, we can gate by region first. Start in us-east only to avoid cross-region differences.

[17:14] Priya Shah: That’s our plan anyway.

[17:16] Marissa Cole: Maybe we should walk through a concrete experiment setup so we’re aligned. Alex, what’s the route name on your side? Is it just our standard /v1/chat/completions?

[17:26] Alex Kim: Yeah, we call your endpoint /v1/chat/completions. We hit it via our service “gateway-chat.” We can add headers there.

[17:35] Logan Wright: Perfect. We’ll normalize the route as the template path, not the raw path. So it should show up as “chat.completions” route in Console.

[17:46] Dana Lopez: Wait, template path? Not sure what that means.

[17:50] Logan Wright: Like if you had /v1/tenants/{id}/chat, we would normalize it to reduce cardinality. For your route, it’s static, so it’s fine.

[18:03] Priya Shah: Okay.

[18:05] Allison Grant: Guardrails suggestion for your first run: p99 TTFT not more than 20% worse than baseline, error rate not more than +0.5 percentage points, cost per 1K output tokens not more than +15%.

[18:22] Priya Shah: Those seem reasonable.

[18:24] Dana Lopez: Why 15% cost?

[18:27] Allison Grant: Just a starting point. If model B is meaningfully better quality, you may accept higher cost. The point is to catch “oops, it doubled output tokens.”

[18:40] Logan Wright: And we can show output tokens by variant so you can see if that’s the driver.

[18:47] Samir Patel: What about CPU / gateway overhead? We sometimes see issues there.

[18:52] Allison Grant: We don’t currently gate on client CPU, but you can correlate with your own. We can expose request queue time, though—if the platform is saturated.

[19:04] Jada Williams: Also: experiments don’t change the capacity model; if you want dedicated GPU pools per variant, that’s a separate configuration.

[19:13] Priya Shah: We’re on hosted right now.

[19:15] Marissa Cole: Yeah, hosted is fine. If you see saturation we can talk dedicated later.

[19:21] Alex Kim: In AWS Bedrock we tried something similar but metrics weren’t separated by variant, which made it useless. So this is good.

[19:30] Marissa Cole: Understood.

[19:31] Dana Lopez: One concern: if we run 90/10, will we have enough data for p99? p99 needs a lot.

[19:38] Logan Wright: Exactly. For small splits, p99 can be noisy. We do show confidence bands? Not yet. But we do show sample sizes. If you want p99 stable, either increase traffic or look at p95 and TTFT.

[19:55] Priya Shah: We can do 95/5 for the first day just to validate, then 80/20.

[20:02] Allison Grant: That’s the typical pattern.

[20:05] Marissa Cole: Let’s talk operational controls. What happens when you stop an experiment?

[20:10] Jada Williams: Stop means the experiment state transitions to “stopping,” then “stopped.” During stopping, the router stops assigning new requests; existing streaming requests can drain. There may be a brief period where old configs still route, due to caching.

[20:29] Samir Patel: Is there a “kill switch” if something is really bad?

[20:33] Jada Williams: Yes, there’s a global kill switch on our side and an account-level flag. In Console you can also hit “Stop immediately,” which bypasses some drain behavior.

[20:44] Priya Shah: Good.

[20:46] Allison Grant: And alerts: we can send you webhooks? Not in beta. But you’ll see in Console, and our CSM team can also notify if we see issues.

[20:58] Marissa Cole: For the beta, we’ll set you up with the standard dashboards internally as well, so if you ping us we can help triage quickly.

[21:06] Alex Kim: Works.

[21:08] Dana Lopez: What’s the “results” view show? Like do you show deltas vs control?

[21:13] Marissa Cole: Yes. It shows baseline (control) and variants, with a delta column. It’s not statistical significance; it’s just difference in metrics. And then a guardrail badge: OK / Warning / Degraded.

[21:28] Priya Shah: So do we need an explicit control variant?

[21:31] Jada Williams: Not necessarily. You can set variant A as baseline. But we recommend include a control variant equal to current policy, then one or more treatment variants. That way you can stop the experiment and baseline is unchanged.

[21:47] Alex Kim: That’s fine.

[21:48] Samir Patel: Another question: retries. If a request is retried, does it keep same variant?

[21:54] Jada Williams: If the retry has the same stickiness key and experiment_id, yes. But if it’s a new request id and no sticky key, it might not. That’s why we want the explicit header.

[22:08] Priya Shah: We’ll do it.

[22:10] Logan Wright: For streaming specifically, make sure your gateway forwards headers on the initial request. We fixed an issue where the experiment tags were missing on streaming retries in one path, but still—best to verify.

[22:26] Alex Kim: We use Envoy in front. It should forward.

[22:29] Samir Patel: It should, but we sometimes strip unknown headers. We might need to allowlist.

[22:34] Alex Kim: Yeah, good catch.

[22:36] Marissa Cole: Great point. Add “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key” and the response headers to your allowlist.

[22:43] Dana Lopez: Are the response headers always returned?

[22:46] Jada Williams: When an experiment is active and the request is eligible, yes. If not eligible, they’re absent.

[22:54] Priya Shah: Eligibility rules—what could make us not eligible?

[22:58] Jada Williams: If you configure only us-east and the request hits eu-west, not eligible. Or if you set “only for certain tenants” and it doesn’t match. There’s also a safety: if the experiment config is unavailable, we default to baseline and no experiment tags.

[23:15] Samir Patel: That would look like attribution drop, right?

[23:18] Logan Wright: It could, but we try to distinguish “not eligible” vs “should have been eligible.” We record a reason code in internal logs. Console doesn’t show the reason code yet.

[23:30] Marissa Cole: If you run into that, we’ll use the debug endpoint on our side.

[23:35] Priya Shah: Okay.

[23:37] Dana Lopez: Can we talk about naming? Like experiment name and variant name—does it impact metric tags? We don’t want cardinality issues.

[23:45] Logan Wright: Great question. Tags use IDs, not free-form names. Names are for Console display. But still, keep names reasonable.

[23:56] Allison Grant: And we cap number of variants in beta. It’s like… three or five. I think it’s five max.

[24:04] Marissa Cole: For your account we’ll keep it at 3 to start.

[24:07] Priya Shah: Two is enough.

[24:09] Marissa Cole: Perfect.

[24:10] Alex Kim: How do we create the experiment? Is it self-serve in Console yet?

[24:14] Marissa Cole: In beta it’s behind a feature flag. Once enabled, you’ll see “Experiments” under Rollouts. You can create via wizard: pick route, define variants, set split, set guardrails.

[24:30] Alex Kim: Can we do it via API?

[24:33] Jada Williams: Yes, there’s an API. But for beta, we recommend Console first so you get validation messages.

[24:39] Samir Patel: Validation like what?

[24:41] Jada Williams: Invalid allocation (doesn’t sum to 100), model incompatibility, overlapping experiments, missing sticky key warning, and guardrails that are too aggressive.

[24:54] Priya Shah: Missing sticky key warning—how does it know?

[24:58] Jada Williams: It’s more like “we strongly recommend you configure and pass a stickiness key.” It can’t fully know until you run traffic. Then it can detect low entropy patterns and skew.

[25:11] Logan Wright: We also have a metric: “unique sticky keys” estimate. It’s approximate.

[25:17] Alex Kim: Nice.

[25:18] Dana Lopez: Metrics again: do you show per-variant tokens in/out?

[25:22] Logan Wright: Yes. Tokens_in, tokens_out, and total tokens. For streaming, tokens_out is computed at end. Sometimes partial streams may show zeros until complete.

[25:36] Dana Lopez: Okay.

[25:37] Priya Shah: One thing: We want to compare cost and latency across models, but sometimes model B is in a different region or uses different hardware. Does your system control for that?

[25:48] Jada Williams: You can pin region or allow multiple. If you allow multiple, then you need to filter by region in results. Console supports drill-down by region.

[26:00] Allison Grant: And we recommend start single region to reduce confounders.

[26:05] Priya Shah: Okay.

[26:06] Marissa Cole: Cool. Let’s address any open concerns. Samir, anything from SRE perspective?

[26:12] Samir Patel: Mostly I want to know what happens when things go bad. Like, what’s the on-call path? Do we page you, do we stop ourselves.

[26:20] Marissa Cole: For beta, you can stop it in Console immediately. Also you can email us; we’ll share an escalation alias. We’ll also monitor internally during your first run.

[26:32] Allison Grant: Also, we’ll set conservative alerting for your account. We don’t want to spam you.

[26:38] Samir Patel: Great.

[26:39] Alex Kim: Can we talk about how cost is computed with caching? Because we sometimes hit prefix cache.

[26:47] Logan Wright: Good one. Cost is based on tokens processed by the model runtime, not just tokens in the prompt. If prefix cache hits, you’ll see reduced compute cost. In Console it may show as lower “effective cost.” But in beta, the cost calculation is still mostly token-based; the cache discount may lag.

[27:08] Alex Kim: So it might not reflect caching right away.

[27:10] Logan Wright: Right. We’re improving it, but for now treat cost as token-based primarily.

[27:18] Priya Shah: That’s okay. We mostly compare models, not caching configs.

[27:23] Dana Lopez: Any plan to show “GPU seconds” type metric?

[27:26] Logan Wright: Not in this beta.

[27:28] Marissa Cole: Post-GA maybe.

[27:31] Priya Shah: I have one more: We’ve been evaluating Together.ai. They have A/B but it’s not route-level; it’s like at the client. We want server-side.

[27:41] Marissa Cole: Yes, ours is server-side at routing, so you don’t need to manage it in client.

[27:47] Alex Kim: Great.

[27:49] Marissa Cole: Alright, if it’s okay, I’ll propose the concrete beta plan.

[27:54] Marissa Cole: Step 1: We enable feature flag for your account for us-east. Step 2: You create experiment on /v1/chat/completions with control = current model, treatment = candidate model. Step 3: Set split 95/5 for 2-4 hours just to validate. Step 4: If attribution is clean, run 80/20 for 24 hours. Step 5: Review results in Console together.

[28:20] Priya Shah: That sounds right.

[28:22] Alex Kim: Yes.

[28:23] Samir Patel: Works.

[28:25] Dana Lopez: We’ll need to make sure our Envoy forwards the headers.

[28:29] Marissa Cole: Yep. We’ll send you the exact header names.

[28:33] Jada Williams: And also: if you can, include a request id header in your logs, plus the returned variant id header. That makes debugging easy.

[28:44] Alex Kim: We do request id already.

[28:46] Logan Wright: Perfect.

[28:48] Priya Shah: When can you enable the flag?

[28:51] Marissa Cole: We can do today or tomorrow morning. I’ll coordinate internally after this call. Probably by end of day PT.

[28:59] Priya Shah: Great.

[29:00] Marissa Cole: Any last questions?

[29:02] Dana Lopez: Just confirming: sticky key can be hashed, and you don’t store it.

[29:07] Logan Wright: Correct.

[29:09] Samir Patel: And if we see skew, what’s the first thing to check?

[29:13] Allison Grant: Check: are you sending the sticky key consistently, is it high entropy, and are the variant response headers present. Then check attribution drop rate. If it’s high, it’s probably header forwarding or retry path.

[29:28] Jada Williams: Also check if your stickiness key is accidentally constant—like “anonymous”—for a lot of users.

[29:35] Alex Kim: Yeah, we’ve had that bug before.

[29:38] Marissa Cole: Alright. Recap and next steps:
- Redwood (me) will enable beta flag for your account, us-east first, and confirm RBAC.
- We’ll send docs for header names, plus a short troubleshooting guide.
- You’ll update Envoy allowlist for the request header and ensure response headers aren’t stripped.
- Then you’ll create the experiment and start with 95/5.

[30:02] Priya Shah: Perfect.

[30:04] Marissa Cole: Great. Thanks everyone. We’ll follow up over email with the enablement details and a proposed time next week to review early results.

[30:12] Priya Shah: Thanks.

[30:13] Alex Kim: Thank you.

[30:14] Dana Lopez: Thanks.

[30:15] Samir Patel: Thanks.

(Transcript ends — recording continued with silence / side chatter that was not captured clearly.)
