[00:00] Marissa Cole: Alright, I think we’re recording. Hi everyone. Thanks for making time. This is the route experiments beta technical deep dive. Goal is to make sure you all are unblocked on stickiness, metrics, and guardrails before we flip the flag.

[00:14] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:16] Marissa Cole: Quick intros? I’m Marissa, product lead for Console and Rollouts.

[00:20] Jada Williams: Jada, I’m the backend architect on the rollouts and the A/B routing layer.

[00:24] Logan Wright: Logan, telemetry lead—metrics, tracing, attribution.

[00:27] Allison Grant: Allison, observability standards, work with SRE on dashboards and alerts.

[00:31] Priya Shah: Priya, head of ML at Acme. Dana is ML eng, Alex is platform, Samir SRE.

[00:37] Alex Kim: Hey.

[00:38] Dana Lopez: Hi.

[00:39] Samir Patel: Hello.

[00:41] Marissa Cole: Great. Priya, do you want to frame what you’re trying to do in the beta?

[00:46] Priya Shah: Yeah. We basically want to A/B model variants on a couple endpoints. Our biggest is chat completions. We want to compare, like, model A vs model B, but we don’t want user A to get different models every request. Also we care about cost. We’re currently doing some of this in-house but it’s… kind of brittle.

[01:06] Marissa Cole: Makes sense. And just to set expectations: in the beta, we’re giving you the route-level experiment with deterministic assignment and traffic splits and the core metrics in Console. We’re not doing full stats significance. You’ll get deltas and guardrail status.

[01:20] Priya Shah: Yep. That’s fine.

[01:22] Marissa Cole: Cool. Jada, can you walk through the assignment and stickiness model?

[01:27] Jada Williams: Sure. So, conceptually: an experiment attaches to a route, like /v1/chat/completions, and has variants—each variant points to a target policy, typically a model + config. Then at request time the policy engine evaluates: is there an active experiment for this route, is the request eligible, and if yes, compute a bucket.

[01:50] Jada Williams: The bucket is deterministic. We take a stickiness key—basically a string—we hash it with experiment_id (and a salt) and we land in [0, 1). Then we map that to your allocation, like 90/10 or 50/50.

[02:05] Alex Kim: When you say stickiness key, what do you expect us to send?

[02:09] Jada Williams: In beta we support a few options. The highest precedence is an explicit header you can send—internally we call it the experiment sticky key header. Sometimes folks mis-hear it as “stick key” or “sticky header.” It’s basically “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key”. If you send that, we use it.

[02:28] Dana Lopez: Is it per experiment or global?

[02:31] Jada Williams: It’s global per request. If you want per-experiment control, you can encode it yourself, like “user123:expABC”, but we usually recommend keep it stable per user or per session. The experiment_id is already in the hash input, so you don’t need to include it.

[02:50] Samir Patel: What if we don’t send it?

[02:52] Jada Williams: Then we try to fall back. If you’re using our SDKs, they can pass through a stable identifier if you provide it. Otherwise the router may fall back to something like an account id or request id. But—and this is important—if we fall back to a low entropy value, you can get skew.

[03:10] Priya Shah: Skew like one variant gets everything?

[03:12] Jada Williams: Exactly. If the fallback key is constant for a lot of traffic, the hash collapses. We’ve added validation and warnings in Console for “missing stickiness key” because we saw that in early testing.

[03:26] Alex Kim: We can send user_id reliably for logged-in traffic. For anonymous, we have session cookie.

[03:33] Jada Williams: That’s perfect. For anonymous, session is usually fine. Just make sure it’s stable across retries and across streaming. There’s a subtlety: streaming requests can have retry paths that don’t copy all headers if your gateway is doing something weird. So we want to verify your stickiness header makes it end-to-end.

[03:52] Logan Wright: And it matters for attribution too. If the assignment changes mid-stream—like first chunk from variant A and retry chunk from “no variant”—it can look like missing attribution.

[04:03] Dana Lopez: Okay. So, “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key”. Are there any other headers? Like can we force a variant for debugging?

[04:11] Jada Williams: In beta, we do have an internal-only override header for support, but we generally don’t expose it externally because it can break the integrity of experiments. What we do expose: response headers that tell you what you got assigned to. So you’ll see something like “X-Redwood-Experiment-Id” and “X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant”. I’m not 100% sure we spelled variant with “Variant-Id” or just “Variant”. Marissa, do you remember?

[04:35] Marissa Cole: In Console and docs it’s “X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant-Id”. But yeah, in some earlier builds it was shortened. We’ll confirm what your account will see when we enable.

[04:45] Alex Kim: That’d be super helpful for logging.

[04:48] Marissa Cole: Yep.

[04:49] Priya Shah: How does this interact with rollouts? Because we do canaries sometimes. Like canary a model version, and also run an experiment.

[04:59] Jada Williams: Good question. The precedence is: experiments evaluate at the route routing layer before fallback policies. So first we decide: is this request in experiment, and which variant. Then within that variant, we still apply standard safety checks—like model compatibility, region availability—and then fallback if necessary. 

[05:19] Samir Patel: So fallback can override the variant?

[05:22] Jada Williams: It can, but we record it. The ideal is fallback is rare. If fallback is happening a lot, your experiment results are hard to interpret.

[05:31] Allison Grant: And we show “fallback rate” and “variant effective traffic” in dashboards. One of the alerts is basically “variant got 0 traffic” or “variant traffic unexpectedly low.”

[05:42] Priya Shah: And canary rollouts—like model version canary—where does that sit?

[05:46] Jada Williams: In terms of policy graph: if your variant points at a rollout policy that itself has canary behavior, you can do it, but it’s easy to make it confusing. For beta we recommend keep variants simple: variant A = model X pinned, variant B = model Y pinned. If you want canary within a variant, we can talk, but I’d keep it out of the first experiment.

[06:10] Marissa Cole: Also, to be transparent, we’re still finalizing some UX copy around “overlapping experiments” and “experiments + rollouts,” but the core routing behavior is stable.

[06:20] Alex Kim: Are overlapping experiments allowed? Like two experiments on same route?

[06:25] Jada Williams: Not in the beta. We block it. One active experiment per route. We might add priority later, but not now.

[06:34] Priya Shah: Good.

[06:36] Marissa Cole: Let’s shift to metrics. Logan, can you outline what you emit and what we’ll see in Console?

[06:43] Logan Wright: Yeah. There are two parts: attribution and aggregation. Attribution means: every request/span has tags: route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region. Then we emit request-level metrics—latency and error—and token-level or generation-level metrics—tokens in/out, cost units.

[07:06] Logan Wright: In Console you’ll see the standard set per variant: p50/p95/p99 latency. Error rate—HTTP 5xx plus a bucket for “model error” like upstream timeouts. Throughput—requests per minute and tokens per second. And cost, which is the most confusing one.

[07:27] Dana Lopez: Yeah, what is “cost” exactly? Is it dollars, tokens, GPU seconds?

[07:32] Logan Wright: In Console today we show “estimated cost” in dollars based on your contracted rates, and “cost per 1K output tokens” as a normalized metric. Under the hood it’s derived from tokens in/out and your price schedule.

[07:48] Priya Shah: We care about cost per request too.

[07:50] Logan Wright: You’ll get that as “cost/request” and “tokens/request.” But keep in mind: if variants produce different output lengths, cost changes, and that’s real. The trick is: you also need to compare quality so you don’t just pick the shorter one.

[08:06] Priya Shah: Right. For quality, we have our own eval set.

[08:09] Marissa Cole: In beta we can attach eval signals if you run through our eval harness, but it’s optional. You can still use your own and just monitor latency/cost/errors.

[08:18] Dana Lopez: We probably will do your harness if it’s not too much work.

[08:22] Marissa Cole: Cool, we can do that later in phase 2 of your beta usage.

[08:27] Samir Patel: On latency, is it end-to-end or model only? Because we have some client-side time too.

[08:33] Logan Wright: What we show is server-side. Specifically: request received at edge to response complete. For streaming we track time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token separately. Sometimes the labels are “TTFT” and “TTLT”.

[08:50] Alex Kim: Good. TTFT is our big KPI.

[08:53] Logan Wright: Yep. For the percentiles we do p50/p95/p99 of TTFT and also overall latency. In the current Console view, you may need to toggle which chart you’re looking at.

[09:07] Allison Grant: And guardrails can be based on p95 or p99 depending on what you choose.

[09:12] Priya Shah: What’s the aggregation window? Like near real-time?

[09:15] Logan Wright: Near real-time is “few minutes” but not instantaneous. The rollup is in 1-minute buckets and then the dashboard shows 5-min or 15-min smoothing. There is sometimes a delay if the aggregation jobs are behind. We also show a “data freshness” indicator.

[09:34] Samir Patel: If the job is behind, does that affect guardrails auto stop?

[09:38] Allison Grant: Good question. Guardrails use the same underlying rollups but with a bit of a conservative window. For beta we treat auto-stop as optional.

[09:48] Marissa Cole: That’s actually a key point: in the beta, default behavior is “soft-stop”—we alert and mark the experiment as degraded, and we recommend you stop it in Console. Hard-stop is available but behind an additional switch.

[10:03] Priya Shah: We prefer soft-stop at first. False positives scare us.

[10:07] Allison Grant: Same. We’ve seen cases where a regional incident triggers p99 spikes for a few minutes and you don’t want everything to stop and flap.

[10:17] Dana Lopez: So what guardrails exist out of the box?

[10:20] Allison Grant: Defaults are: p99 latency regression threshold, error rate threshold, and cost per 1K tokens threshold. Also an “attribution drop rate” guardrail, which is more of a data quality thing.

[10:34] Alex Kim: What’s attribution drop rate?

[10:37] Logan Wright: It’s the percentage of requests on a route that should have experiment_id/variant_id but don’t. Like if the router assigned variant A but the spans downstream didn’t carry the tags, or streaming retry paths lost context.

[10:52] Jada Williams: Or if the client didn’t send the sticky key and the router fell back weirdly—some requests might not be considered eligible or might be in “control” without being tagged as control.

[11:06] Priya Shah: So basically “your measurement is broken.”

[11:08] Logan Wright: Exactly.

[11:10] Samir Patel: What thresholds do you use?

[11:13] Allison Grant: For beta, we generally start warning at 0.5% and alert at 2%, but it depends on volume. We can tune per account. Also we treat it as “do not trust results” rather than “service is down.”

[11:28] Samir Patel: Okay.

[11:30] Marissa Cole: On guardrail semantics: you can configure “stop experiment” vs “notify only.” Stop experiment in beta means stop routing traffic to variants and revert to your baseline policy.

[11:43] Alex Kim: Is it immediate?

[11:45] Jada Williams: It’s near-immediate, but there is config propagation. We have an SLO for config staleness; typical is under a minute, worst case a few minutes. There’s a propagation lag metric.

[12:00] Samir Patel: So you might “stop” but still see some variant traffic for a bit.

[12:03] Jada Williams: Exactly. And we log the config version used for assignment.

[12:10] Priya Shah: Does Console show that? Like the config version?

[12:13] Marissa Cole: In the admin/debug view, yes. For most users it’s hidden.

[12:18] Alex Kim: We’d want it for troubleshooting.

[12:21] Marissa Cole: We can grant the right RBAC role. There’s a permission for “view experiment debug details.” It’s not fully self-serve yet but we can set it up.

[12:33] Dana Lopez: What’s the recommended ramp plan?

[12:36] Allison Grant: We recommend: create experiment at 0% first (or 1% depending on UI), validate attribution and variant headers, then ramp 1% → 5% → 10% → 25% with at least a few hours between steps. For high-risk routes, 24 hours at 10%.

[12:58] Priya Shah: We can do 1% for a day. Our traffic is high.

[13:02] Logan Wright: High traffic makes the metrics more stable, which is nice.

[13:06] Alex Kim: Can we split by region? Like do experiment only in us-east first.

[13:11] Jada Williams: Yes, there are eligibility rules. In beta we support a simple region allowlist and maybe a header-based rule. If you want “only these tenants” we can do that too, but we should define the key.

[13:27] Priya Shah: Start with region only.

[13:30] Marissa Cole: That’s easiest.

[13:32] Dana Lopez: How do we define the variants? Is it only model id, or can we change parameters too?

[13:38] Jada Williams: Variant can point to a full routing target: model + quantization profile + batching config, basically whatever you can configure today for a route policy. But again, for interpretability, change one thing at a time.

[13:55] Priya Shah: We want to compare two model IDs first.

[13:58] Jada Williams: Great.

[14:00] Marissa Cole: Another question we’ve gotten is: what about users switching between variants when traffic split changes. Assignment is deterministic; traffic split just changes cut points. That means if you change from 90/10 to 50/50, some users will “move” variants.

[14:19] Alex Kim: That’s okay.

[14:20] Priya Shah: Yeah, expected.

[14:22] Samir Patel: Is there any “sticky” guarantee across days if the experiment restarts?

[14:27] Jada Williams: If you keep experiment_id the same, yes. If you stop and create a new experiment, no. So if you want continuity, pause rather than delete.

[14:40] Priya Shah: Makes sense.

[14:41] Logan Wright: Also: for cost metrics, make sure you understand if you’re looking at “per request” vs “per token.” Some teams accidentally optimize “cost/request” by forcing shorter responses but quality drops.

[14:55] Priya Shah: We’ve done that mistake before.

[14:57] Dana Lopez: Yeah.

[15:00] Marissa Cole: We can talk about quality signals briefly. Priya, do you want Redwood eval integration now or later?

[15:07] Priya Shah: Later. We have internal scoring and we’re not ready to push prompts outside that system yet.

[15:14] Marissa Cole: Totally fine. We can just start with latency/cost/errors and manual review.

[15:20] Alex Kim: On privacy: the stickiness key header—should it be hashed? We can’t send raw user id sometimes.

[15:28] Jada Williams: It can be hashed. We treat it as opaque. Just keep it stable. Use a keyed hash if you want.

[15:37] Samir Patel: Do you store it?

[15:39] Logan Wright: We do not store the raw sticky key in metrics. We use it at request time for bucketing. In logs, we also avoid recording it by default. The debug endpoint can show “hash input present” but it doesn’t echo the full key.

[15:54] Marissa Cole: And audit logs: experiment config changes are captured—who created, started, updated split, stopped, etc.

[16:03] Priya Shah: Good. That matters for us.

[16:06] Samir Patel: On audit logs, are they exportable? Like to our SIEM.

[16:11] Marissa Cole: In GA yes; in beta we have a basic export and we can provide it on request. We’ll follow up with the exact fields. For now, you’ll at least have the change history in Console.

[16:26] Alex Kim: Okay.

[16:28] Dana Lopez: One more on deterministic bucketing: what hash algorithm? Is it consistent across regions?

[16:34] Jada Williams: We use a stable hash—murmur-style, not crypto—plus a salt. It’s consistent across regions as long as the same config version is present. That’s why propagation lag matters. But the algorithm itself is consistent.

[16:51] Priya Shah: Great.

[16:52] Samir Patel: If we have multi-region active-active, can the same user hit different region and get different variant because of config lag?

[17:00] Jada Williams: In theory yes, during propagation windows. Practically, we keep it tight. If you want stricter, we can gate by region first. Start in us-east only to avoid cross-region differences.

[17:14] Priya Shah: That’s our plan anyway.

[17:16] Marissa Cole: Maybe we should walk through a concrete experiment setup so we’re aligned. Alex, what’s the route name on your side? Is it just our standard /v1/chat/completions?

[17:26] Alex Kim: Yeah, we call your endpoint /v1/chat/completions. We hit it via our service “gateway-chat.” We can add headers there.

[17:35] Logan Wright: Perfect. We’ll normalize the route as the template path, not the raw path. So it should show up as “chat.completions” route in Console.

[17:46] Dana Lopez: Wait, template path? Not sure what that means.

[17:50] Logan Wright: Like if you had /v1/tenants/{id}/chat, we would normalize it to reduce cardinality. For your route, it’s static, so it’s fine.

[18:03] Priya Shah: Okay.

[18:05] Allison Grant: Guardrails suggestion for your first run: p99 TTFT not more than 20% worse than baseline, error rate not more than +0.5 percentage points, cost per 1K output tokens not more than +15%.

[18:22] Priya Shah: Those seem reasonable.

[18:24] Dana Lopez: Why 15% cost?

[18:27] Allison Grant: Just a starting point. If model B is meaningfully better quality, you may accept higher cost. The point is to catch “oops, it doubled output tokens.”

[18:40] Logan Wright: And we can show output tokens by variant so you can see if that’s the driver.

[18:47] Samir Patel: What about CPU / gateway overhead? We sometimes see issues there.

[18:52] Allison Grant: We don’t currently gate on client CPU, but you can correlate with your own. We can expose request queue time, though—if the platform is saturated.

[19:04] Jada Williams: Also: experiments don’t change the capacity model; if you want dedicated GPU pools per variant, that’s a separate configuration.

[19:13] Priya Shah: We’re on hosted right now.

[19:15] Marissa Cole: Yeah, hosted is fine. If you see saturation we can talk dedicated later.

[19:21] Alex Kim: In AWS Bedrock we tried something similar but metrics weren’t separated by variant, which made it useless. So this is good.

[19:30] Marissa Cole: Understood.

[19:31] Dana Lopez: One concern: if we run 90/10, will we have enough data for p99? p99 needs a lot.

[19:38] Logan Wright: Exactly. For small splits, p99 can be noisy. We do show confidence bands? Not yet. But we do show sample sizes. If you want p99 stable, either increase traffic or look at p95 and TTFT.

[19:55] Priya Shah: We can do 95/5 for the first day just to validate, then 80/20.

[20:02] Allison Grant: That’s the typical pattern.

[20:05] Marissa Cole: Let’s talk operational controls. What happens when you stop an experiment?

[20:10] Jada Williams: Stop means the experiment state transitions to “stopping,” then “stopped.” During stopping, the router stops assigning new requests; existing streaming requests can drain. There may be a brief period where old configs still route, due to caching.

[20:29] Samir Patel: Is there a “kill switch” if something is really bad?

[20:33] Jada Williams: Yes, there’s a global kill switch on our side and an account-level flag. In Console you can also hit “Stop immediately,” which bypasses some drain behavior.

[20:44] Priya Shah: Good.

[20:46] Allison Grant: And alerts: we can send you webhooks? Not in beta. But you’ll see in Console, and our CSM team can also notify if we see issues.

[20:58] Marissa Cole: For the beta, we’ll set you up with the standard dashboards internally as well, so if you ping us we can help triage quickly.

[21:06] Alex Kim: Works.

[21:08] Dana Lopez: What’s the “results” view show? Like do you show deltas vs control?

[21:13] Marissa Cole: Yes. It shows baseline (control) and variants, with a delta column. It’s not statistical significance; it’s just difference in metrics. And then a guardrail badge: OK / Warning / Degraded.

[21:28] Priya Shah: So do we need an explicit control variant?

[21:31] Jada Williams: Not necessarily. You can set variant A as baseline. But we recommend include a control variant equal to current policy, then one or more treatment variants. That way you can stop the experiment and baseline is unchanged.

[21:47] Alex Kim: That’s fine.

[21:48] Samir Patel: Another question: retries. If a request is retried, does it keep same variant?

[21:54] Jada Williams: If the retry has the same stickiness key and experiment_id, yes. But if it’s a new request id and no sticky key, it might not. That’s why we want the explicit header.

[22:08] Priya Shah: We’ll do it.

[22:10] Logan Wright: For streaming specifically, make sure your gateway forwards headers on the initial request. We fixed an issue where the experiment tags were missing on streaming retries in one path, but still—best to verify.

[22:26] Alex Kim: We use Envoy in front. It should forward.

[22:29] Samir Patel: It should, but we sometimes strip unknown headers. We might need to allowlist.

[22:34] Alex Kim: Yeah, good catch.

[22:36] Marissa Cole: Great point. Add “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key” and the response headers to your allowlist.

[22:43] Dana Lopez: Are the response headers always returned?

[22:46] Jada Williams: When an experiment is active and the request is eligible, yes. If not eligible, they’re absent.

[22:54] Priya Shah: Eligibility rules—what could make us not eligible?

[22:58] Jada Williams: If you configure only us-east and the request hits eu-west, not eligible. Or if you set “only for certain tenants” and it doesn’t match. There’s also a safety: if the experiment config is unavailable, we default to baseline and no experiment tags.

[23:15] Samir Patel: That would look like attribution drop, right?

[23:18] Logan Wright: It could, but we try to distinguish “not eligible” vs “should have been eligible.” We record a reason code in internal logs. Console doesn’t show the reason code yet.

[23:30] Marissa Cole: If you run into that, we’ll use the debug endpoint on our side.

[23:35] Priya Shah: Okay.

[23:37] Dana Lopez: Can we talk about naming? Like experiment name and variant name—does it impact metric tags? We don’t want cardinality issues.

[23:45] Logan Wright: Great question. Tags use IDs, not free-form names. Names are for Console display. But still, keep names reasonable.

[23:56] Allison Grant: And we cap number of variants in beta. It’s like… three or five. I think it’s five max.

[24:04] Marissa Cole: For your account we’ll keep it at 3 to start.

[24:07] Priya Shah: Two is enough.

[24:09] Marissa Cole: Perfect.

[24:10] Alex Kim: How do we create the experiment? Is it self-serve in Console yet?

[24:14] Marissa Cole: In beta it’s behind a feature flag. Once enabled, you’ll see “Experiments” under Rollouts. You can create via wizard: pick route, define variants, set split, set guardrails.

[24:30] Alex Kim: Can we do it via API?

[24:33] Jada Williams: Yes, there’s an API. But for beta, we recommend Console first so you get validation messages.

[24:39] Samir Patel: Validation like what?

[24:41] Jada Williams: Invalid allocation (doesn’t sum to 100), model incompatibility, overlapping experiments, missing sticky key warning, and guardrails that are too aggressive.

[24:54] Priya Shah: Missing sticky key warning—how does it know?

[24:58] Jada Williams: It’s more like “we strongly recommend you configure and pass a stickiness key.” It can’t fully know until you run traffic. Then it can detect low entropy patterns and skew.

[25:11] Logan Wright: We also have a metric: “unique sticky keys” estimate. It’s approximate.

[25:17] Alex Kim: Nice.

[25:18] Dana Lopez: Metrics again: do you show per-variant tokens in/out?

[25:22] Logan Wright: Yes. Tokens_in, tokens_out, and total tokens. For streaming, tokens_out is computed at end. Sometimes partial streams may show zeros until complete.

[25:36] Dana Lopez: Okay.

[25:37] Priya Shah: One thing: We want to compare cost and latency across models, but sometimes model B is in a different region or uses different hardware. Does your system control for that?

[25:48] Jada Williams: You can pin region or allow multiple. If you allow multiple, then you need to filter by region in results. Console supports drill-down by region.

[26:00] Allison Grant: And we recommend start single region to reduce confounders.

[26:05] Priya Shah: Okay.

[26:06] Marissa Cole: Cool. Let’s address any open concerns. Samir, anything from SRE perspective?

[26:12] Samir Patel: Mostly I want to know what happens when things go bad. Like, what’s the on-call path? Do we page you, do we stop ourselves.

[26:20] Marissa Cole: For beta, you can stop it in Console immediately. Also you can email us; we’ll share an escalation alias. We’ll also monitor internally during your first run.

[26:32] Allison Grant: Also, we’ll set conservative alerting for your account. We don’t want to spam you.

[26:38] Samir Patel: Great.

[26:39] Alex Kim: Can we talk about how cost is computed with caching? Because we sometimes hit prefix cache.

[26:47] Logan Wright: Good one. Cost is based on tokens processed by the model runtime, not just tokens in the prompt. If prefix cache hits, you’ll see reduced compute cost. In Console it may show as lower “effective cost.” But in beta, the cost calculation is still mostly token-based; the cache discount may lag.

[27:08] Alex Kim: So it might not reflect caching right away.

[27:10] Logan Wright: Right. We’re improving it, but for now treat cost as token-based primarily.

[27:18] Priya Shah: That’s okay. We mostly compare models, not caching configs.

[27:23] Dana Lopez: Any plan to show “GPU seconds” type metric?

[27:26] Logan Wright: Not in this beta.

[27:28] Marissa Cole: Post-GA maybe.

[27:31] Priya Shah: I have one more: We’ve been evaluating Together.ai. They have A/B but it’s not route-level; it’s like at the client. We want server-side.

[27:41] Marissa Cole: Yes, ours is server-side at routing, so you don’t need to manage it in client.

[27:47] Alex Kim: Great.

[27:49] Marissa Cole: Alright, if it’s okay, I’ll propose the concrete beta plan.

[27:54] Marissa Cole: Step 1: We enable feature flag for your account for us-east. Step 2: You create experiment on /v1/chat/completions with control = current model, treatment = candidate model. Step 3: Set split 95/5 for 2-4 hours just to validate. Step 4: If attribution is clean, run 80/20 for 24 hours. Step 5: Review results in Console together.

[28:20] Priya Shah: That sounds right.

[28:22] Alex Kim: Yes.

[28:23] Samir Patel: Works.

[28:25] Dana Lopez: We’ll need to make sure our Envoy forwards the headers.

[28:29] Marissa Cole: Yep. We’ll send you the exact header names.

[28:33] Jada Williams: And also: if you can, include a request id header in your logs, plus the returned variant id header. That makes debugging easy.

[28:44] Alex Kim: We do request id already.

[28:46] Logan Wright: Perfect.

[28:48] Priya Shah: When can you enable the flag?

[28:51] Marissa Cole: We can do today or tomorrow morning. I’ll coordinate internally after this call. Probably by end of day PT.

[28:59] Priya Shah: Great.

[29:00] Marissa Cole: Any last questions?

[29:02] Dana Lopez: Just confirming: sticky key can be hashed, and you don’t store it.

[29:07] Logan Wright: Correct.

[29:09] Samir Patel: And if we see skew, what’s the first thing to check?

[29:13] Allison Grant: Check: are you sending the sticky key consistently, is it high entropy, and are the variant response headers present. Then check attribution drop rate. If it’s high, it’s probably header forwarding or retry path.

[29:28] Jada Williams: Also check if your stickiness key is accidentally constant—like “anonymous”—for a lot of users.

[29:35] Alex Kim: Yeah, we’ve had that bug before.

[29:38] Marissa Cole: Alright. Recap and next steps:
- Redwood (me) will enable beta flag for your account, us-east first, and confirm RBAC.
- We’ll send docs for header names, plus a short troubleshooting guide.
- You’ll update Envoy allowlist for the request header and ensure response headers aren’t stripped.
- Then you’ll create the experiment and start with 95/5.

[30:02] Priya Shah: Perfect.

[30:04] Marissa Cole: Great. Thanks everyone. We’ll follow up over email with the enablement details and a proposed time next week to review early results.

[30:12] Priya Shah: Thanks.

[30:13] Alex Kim: Thank you.

[30:14] Dana Lopez: Thanks.

[30:15] Samir Patel: Thanks.

(Transcript ends — recording continued with silence / side chatter that was not captured clearly.)
text:
Fireflies.ai transcription

Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Route Experiments (A/B) beta technical deep dive
Date: 2025-02-06
Time: 10:02 AM PT
Duration: 58 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Marissa Cole, Jada Williams, Logan Wright, Allison Grant
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Alex Kim, Dana Lopez, Samir Patel

Summary (auto-generated)
- Walked through route-level experiments beta: how deterministic assignment works, what to use for stickiness keys, and how experiment precedence interacts with rollouts/fallback.
- Reviewed metrics and attribution: latency percentiles, error rate, throughput, and cost/token definitions; discussed the “attribution drop rate” failure mode.
- Covered guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop behavior, default thresholds, and recommended ramp plan.
- Agreed on next steps to enable the beta flag and start a small ramp on Acme’s primary chat route.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Stickiness keys and deterministic bucketing
- Variant configuration and traffic splits
- Metrics definitions (latency, cost, errors) and attribution schema
- Guardrails, rollback triggers, and operating experiments safely
- Beta enablement and timeline

Transcript

[00:00] Marissa Cole: Alright, I think we’re recording. Hi everyone. Thanks for making time. This is the route experiments beta technical deep dive. Goal is to make sure you all are unblocked on stickiness, metrics, and guardrails before we flip the flag.

[00:14] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:16] Marissa Cole: Quick intros? I’m Marissa, product lead for Console and Rollouts.

[00:20] Jada Williams: Jada, I’m the backend architect on the rollouts and the A/B routing layer.

[00:24] Logan Wright: Logan, telemetry lead—metrics, tracing, attribution.

[00:27] Allison Grant: Allison, observability standards, work with SRE on dashboards and alerts.

[00:31] Priya Shah: Priya, head of ML at Acme. Dana is ML eng, Alex is platform, Samir SRE.

[00:37] Alex Kim: Hey.

[00:38] Dana Lopez: Hi.

[00:39] Samir Patel: Hello.

[00:41] Marissa Cole: Great. Priya, do you want to frame what you’re trying to do in the beta?

[00:46] Priya Shah: Yeah. We basically want to A/B model variants on a couple endpoints. Our biggest is chat completions. We want to compare, like, model A vs model B, but we don’t want user A to get different models every request. Also we care about cost. We’re currently doing some of this in-house but it’s… kind of brittle.

[01:06] Marissa Cole: Makes sense. And just to set expectations: in the beta, we’re giving you the route-level experiment with deterministic assignment and traffic splits and the core metrics in Console. We’re not doing full stats significance. You’ll get deltas and guardrail status.

[01:20] Priya Shah: Yep. That’s fine.

[01:22] Marissa Cole: Cool. Jada, can you walk through the assignment and stickiness model?

[01:27] Jada Williams: Sure. So, conceptually: an experiment attaches to a route, like /v1/chat/completions, and has variants—each variant points to a target policy, typically a model + config. Then at request time the policy engine evaluates: is there an active experiment for this route, is the request eligible, and if yes, compute a bucket.

[01:50] Jada Williams: The bucket is deterministic. We take a stickiness key—basically a string—we hash it with experiment_id (and a salt) and we land in [0, 1). Then we map that to your allocation, like 90/10 or 50/50.

[02:05] Alex Kim: When you say stickiness key, what do you expect us to send?

[02:09] Jada Williams: In beta we support a few options. The highest precedence is an explicit header you can send—internally we call it the experiment sticky key header. Sometimes folks mis-hear it as “stick key” or “sticky header.” It’s basically “X-Redwood-Sticky-Key”. If you send that, we use it.

[02:28] Dana Lopez: Is it per experiment or global?

[02:31] Jada Williams: It’s global per request. If you want per-experiment control, you can encode it yourself, like “user123:expABC”, but we usually recommend keep it stable per user or per session. The experiment_id is already in the hash input, so you don’t need to include it.

[02:50] Samir Patel: What if we don’t send it?
